Explaination: User-controlled XML text flows into Statement.execute(), which is SQL execution, not OS command execution; still a real injection path but for SQL injection, not CWE-078.

response.analysis: The reported dataflow is real, but it is not a CWE-078 issue. `ResultsReporter.build()` parses a report XML, `getSql()` and `buildReport()` extract `<sql>` text, and the resulting strings are passed into JDBC `Statement.execute(...)` / `Statement.executeQuery(...)`. That means the sink is SQL execution against the configured database, not OS command execution. There is no `Runtime.exec`, `ProcessBuilder`, shell invocation, or similar command-execution API on this path. The path is also part of the intended report-generation design: the `-rf` CLI option is explicitly documented as an XML file specifying SQL for reports, and when `-rf` is absent the code loads bundled trusted XML resources. So the path is reachable, but the selected sink does not match CWE-078. This is primarily a source/sink misidentification problem (B1), not an unreachable-path problem (A1/A2).
response.evidence: `ResultsReporter.java:66-67` defines `-rf` as an `xml specifying sql to call for the reports`. `ResultsReporter.java:85-115` parses the XML file; `ResultsReporter.java:195-205` does `ret.add(child.getTextContent())`; `ResultsReporter.java:138-142` assigns `r.sql = child.getTextContent()`. `ResultsReporter.java:248-253` shows the XML comes either from bundled defaults or an operator-supplied `-rf` file. The actual sinks are JDBC only: `ResultsReporter.java:310-329` calls `st.execute(sql)` for `before/after`, and `Report.java:93-96` calls `st.executeQuery(sql)` for report SQL. The bundled config confirms this is intentional SQL configuration, e.g. `comparison-reports.xml:25-29` contains `<sql>drop index if exists ...</sql>` and `<sql>create index ...</sql>`. A search in the reporting code finds JDBC execute methods but no OS command APIs on this path. Therefore, even if an attacker controlled the XML, the resulting behavior would be database SQL execution, not OS command injection under CWE-078.

response.analysis: Source/sink check: the report treats the `InputStream` parameter as if it becomes OS-command text, but in `ExternalEmbedder.embed()` it only contributes a filename that is substituted into a preconfigured command template and then executed with `Runtime.exec(String[])` / `Runtime.exec(String)`, not through a shell. That does not satisfy CWE-078 by itself because shell metacharacters in a filename are not interpreted as new OS commands. Path check: for the normal case of a plain `InputStream`, `TikaInputStream.get(inputStream)` creates a `TikaInputStream` with `path == null`, and `getFile()` later spools the bytes to a Tika-created temp file, so the command receives a library-generated temp path rather than attacker-controlled command content. The report also over-approximates the generic `InputStream` source by following the special branch where the caller already supplied a `TikaInputStream` carrying an existing `Path`. Even in that branch, the value is still only a process argument, not a shell command. Therefore this report is a false positive for CWE-078.
response.evidence: ExternalEmbedder.java:358-360 replaces `${INPUT}` with `tikaInputStream.getFile().toString()`, and lines 403-406 execute the result via `Runtime.getRuntime().exec(...)`. Explaination: User-controlled InputStream can influence the file path passed into ProcessBuilder. Although args are separated, invoking an external process with untrusted path data is a relevant command-execution risk path.

response.analysis: The report is a false positive, primarily due to B1 (incorrect sink selection). The path is broadly reachable when `MagikaDetector` is enabled, but the reported flow does not satisfy CWE-078. `MagikaDetector.detectOnPath()` passes a fixed executable (`magikaPath`) and a separate file-path argument into `new ProcessBuilder(String[])`. In Java, this is a direct process launch, not shell execution, so the attacker-controlled value does not become part of a shell command string. At most, the input influences which file `magika` analyzes. That is not command injection. The code itself identifies the real dangerous input as `magikaPath` if an untrusted user can set it, but that is configuration input, not the reported `InputStream`. Also, for non-file-backed streams, `TikaInputStream.getPath()` spools to a Tika-generated temporary file, so the stream does not concretely control the executed command structure or filename beyond normal file content processing. The report's explanation overgeneralizes process invocation with untrusted arguments into CWE-078.
response.evidence:
Relevant sink code in `tika-detectors/tika-detector-magika/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/detect/magika/MagikaDetector.java`:
`String[] args = new String[]{`
`    ProcessUtils.escapeCommandLine(magikaPath),`
`    ProcessUtils.escapeCommandLine(path.toAbsolutePath().toString()),`
`    "--json"`
`};`
`ProcessBuilder builder = new ProcessBuilder(args);`
Here the executable is `args[0]` (`magikaPath`) and the reported taint only reaches `args[1]`, a single file argument. There is no shell involved and no concatenated command string.

The same class explicitly marks the real risky input as the executable path, not the document stream:
`public void setMagikaPath(String fileCommandPath) {`
`    //this opens up a potential command vulnerability.`
`    //Don't ever let an untrusted user set this.`
`    this.magikaPath = fileCommandPath;`
This strongly indicates that the command-execution risk is from untrusted configuration of `magikaPath`, which is not the reported source.

The path is also less attacker-controlled than the report claims. In `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java`, `getPath()` returns the original backing path only if one already exists; otherwise it creates a temp file:
`if (path != null) { return path; }`
`Path tmpFile = tmp.createTempFile(suffix);`
And `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TemporaryResources.java` uses:
`Files.createTempFile("apache-tika-", actualSuffix)`
So normal uploaded stream bytes are spooled to a Tika-generated temp path.

Even the temp suffix is constrained. `TikaInputStream.getExtension(metadata)` calls `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath(name)`, and `FilenameUtils` only accepts suffixes matching `\A\.(?i)[a-z0-9]{1,5}\Z`. Combined with `path.toAbsolutePath().toString()`, a malicious filename like `--json` would become an absolute filesystem path such as `/tmp/--json`, not a new command or option. This can affect which file is read, but it does not create a CWE-078 command-injection primitive.

response.analysis: The reported flow is real and reachable, but it does not produce CWE-078. `ResultsReporter.build()` parses report XML, extracts `<sql>` text with `getTextContent()`, stores it in `before`/`after` lists or `Report.sql`, and later `execute()` / `executeQuery()` send those strings to JDBC. That is SQL execution, not OS command construction or execution. No code on this path calls `Runtime.exec`, `ProcessBuilder`, a shell, or any other OS-command sink. In the real usage model, this tool is explicitly designed to accept a report XML file that specifies SQL to run, and the bundled default report files contain many predefined `<sql>` blocks. So even though untrusted use of `-rf` could let an operator supply arbitrary SQL, this is a trusted-script/report mechanism, not a CWE-078 OS command injection. The FP is therefore not due to unreachable control flow or shallow path construction; it is due to choosing the wrong sink/CWE.
response.evidence: Source/dataflow: `ResultsReporter.main()` accepts `-rf` as a reports file (`ResultsReporter.java:66-67, 248-253`). `build()` parses that XML (`85-115`), `getSql()` copies each child element text via `child.getTextContent()` (`195-207`), `addBefore/addAfter` store it (`298-303`), and `execute()` runs each string using `Statement st = c.createStatement(); ... st.execute(sql);` (`310-329`). For per-report SQL, `buildReport()` assigns `r.sql = child.getTextContent()` (`138-142`), and `Report.dumpReportToWorkbook()` executes it with `st.executeQuery(sql)` (`Report.java:93-95`). These are JDBC SQL sinks only. Intended-use evidence: the CLI help explicitly documents `-rf` as `xml specifying sql to call for the reports` (`ResultsReporter.java:66-67`), and bundled configs such as `profile-reports.xml:21-41` and `comparison-reports.xml:25-60` contain numerous `<sql>` statements by design.
response.new_reason:: Secondary observation: the report explanation itself acknowledges that the sink is SQL execution rather than OS command execution, which also reflects a CWE-definition mismatch (C1).

Explaination: User-controlled argument list is passed into a Docker command invocation via addArgs(...) and then executed. This can influence process execution and is a plausible command/code injection path.

response.analysis: The reported path is reachable, but it does not represent a real CWE-094 in OneDev. In `ServerDockerExecutor.runStepContainer(...)`, the code builds a structured `docker run` argument vector and appends the reported user-controlled data only as Docker/container arguments after the image. That is intended CI behavior: these values are supposed to become the command/argv executed inside the build container, not host-side code unexpectedly interpreted by OneDev. `RunContainerStep` explicitly defines this source as container `Arguments`, and command steps with an image are intentionally routed through the Docker executor. OneDev also has a separate `ServerShellExecutor` for actual host-shell execution, and that path is explicitly documented as privileged. So the report confuses intended container command execution with a host command/code injection bug.
response.evidence: `ServerDockerExecutor.java:380-440` shows the sink construction: `docker.addArgs("run", ...)`, optional Docker flags, optional `--entrypoint=...`, then `docker.addArgs(image); docker.addArgs(arguments.toArray(new String[0]));`. This means the tainted data is passed as post-image container argv, not as a host shell command string. `ServerDockerExecutor.java:471-487` shows the `entrypoint.arguments()` flow is used only for `CommandFacade` steps that have an image, meaning they are containerized command steps by design. `RunContainerStep.java:63-65` documents the source as `Specify container arguments ... do not confuse this with container options`, confirming intended semantics. In contrast, `ServerShellExecutor.java:65-68` explicitly warns that jobs run with server-process permissions, and `ServerShellExecutor.java:174-194` writes a script file and invokes an interpreter on the host; that is the real host command-execution path. The Docker executor path does not create unintended code execution in OneDev itself; at most it runs the job author's chosen command inside the job container or fails the step. Any host-level risk would require separate privileged settings such as mounting `/var/run/docker.sock` (`ServerDockerExecutor.java:194-198`), which is already explicitly warned about and is not caused by this `arguments` flow.

response.analysis: The reported source-to-sink flow is real and reachable: `MessageListenerServlet` reads `request.getHeader(WebClient.selectorName)` and passes it through `WebClient.getConsumer(...)` to `Session.createConsumer(destination, selector)`. So this is not an A1/A2 reachability mistake. The false positive is that the sink is not a CWE-094 sink. In ActiveMQ, the second argument is a JMS message selector, i.e. a constrained SQL-92-like filter expression used to decide which messages a consumer receives. `ActiveMQMessageConsumer` validates it with `SelectorParser.parse(selector)`, and the broker converts it to a `BooleanExpression` and evaluates `selectorExpression.matches(context)` during message filtering. No code is generated or executed from the header value. In the reported `MessageListenerServlet` path, malformed selectors only raise `JMSException`, which is caught and logged, so the practical effect is a failed subscription/request, not code execution or a meaningful crash. The report therefore misclassifies intended selector input as CWE-094.
response.evidence: Relevant path: `activemq-web/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/web/MessageListenerServlet.java` lines 165 and 176 call `client.getConsumer(destination, request.getHeader(WebClient.selectorName))`; `activemq-web/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/web/WebClient.java` line 337 calls `getSession().createConsumer(destination, selector)`. Sink semantics: `activemq-client/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/ActiveMQMessageConsumer.java` lines 261-264 perform `SelectorParser.parse(selector); this.info.setSelector(selector);`; `activemq-client/src/main/grammar/SelectorParser.jj` lines 72-95 parse the selector into a cached `BooleanExpression` or throw `InvalidSelectorException`; `activemq-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/broker/region/AbstractSubscription.java` lines 72-76 parse `info.getSelector()` and line 109 evaluates `(selectorExpression == null || selectorExpression.matches(context))`. Intended feature evidence: `activemq-web/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/web/QueueBrowseServlet.java` lines 45-47 document `selector` as `the SQL 92 selector to apply to the queue`. Error handling on the reported path: `MessageListenerServlet` wraps consumer creation in `catch (JMSException e)`, logging the error instead of executing user input.

response.analysis: The reported dataflow from an HTTP header into a JMS selector is real, but it does not constitute CWE-094 in this codebase. Source analysis: `MessageServlet.doMessages` reads a user-controlled header via `request.getHeader(WebClient.selectorName)` and passes it to `client.getConsumer(...)` (`activemq-web/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/web/MessageServlet.java:185`). `WebClient.selectorName` defaults to `selector` (`activemq-web/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/web/WebClient.java:126-130`), and `WebClient.getConsumer` forwards the string to `getSession().createConsumer(destination, selector)` (`activemq-web/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/web/WebClient.java:334-338`). Sink analysis: in ActiveMQ, this selector is parsed as a JMS selector expression, not compiled/generated as Java code. `ActiveMQMessageConsumer` explicitly validates it with `SelectorParser.parse(selector)` before storing it (`activemq-client/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/ActiveMQMessageConsumer.java:261-265`). `SelectorParser` builds a constrained `BooleanExpression` AST from a fixed grammar (`activemq-client/src/main/grammar/SelectorParser.jj:72-95`), so the sink is not a code-injection/code-generation sink for CWE-094. The report explanation itself says this is 'not code execution', which already conflicts with CWE-094. Realistically, an attacker may influence which messages are consumed or trigger an `InvalidSelectorException` for malformed selectors, causing request failure, but not arbitrary code execution. The report also appends `activemq-jms-pool/PooledSession.createConsumer` as part of the path, but `activemq-web` uses `ActiveMQConnectionFactory` directly (`activemq-web/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/web/WebClient.java:268-289`), so that tail is not the concrete project path here.
response.evidence: Concrete flow exists: `MessageServlet.java:185` -> `WebClient.java:334-338` -> JMS `createConsumer(destination, selector)`. However, `ActiveMQMessageConsumer.java:261-265` validates the selector with `SelectorParser.parse(selector)`. `SelectorParser.jj:72-95` shows the input is parsed into a `BooleanExpression` using a fixed selector grammar, not executed as generated code. Built-in selector functions are limited and predefined (`BuiltinFunctionRegistry.java:29-34`: `INLIST`, `MAKELIST`, `REGEX`, `REPLACE`, `SPLIT`), and there is no project-specific custom function registration (`rg registerFunction` only finds the registry itself). Even advanced selector forms are evaluator-based rather than code execution primitives: `XQueryExpression.evaluate` simply returns `Boolean.FALSE` (`XQueryExpression.java:32-33`), and `XPathExpression` uses a secured XML parser configuration with secure-processing and external entity restrictions (`XPathExpression.java:58-67`). Invalid input would raise `InvalidSelectorException` during parse/consumer creation, which `MessageServlet` wraps as `ServletException` (`MessageServlet.java:176-185`, `214-220`, catch block below), yielding a failed request rather than code execution. Therefore the selected sink is valid for generic selector/filter influence, but not for CWE-094.
response.new_reason:: C1 also applies at the report level: the explanation explicitly characterizes the issue as selector/JMS injection and 'not code execution', which deviates from the target CWE-094 definition. The primary key reason, however, is still B1 because the chosen sink is not a CWE-094 sink.

However, the report is a false positive because the source is not an attacker-reachable input in the real product. Full-project context shows this code is part of the Joram JMS test harness, not broker/runtime request handling. The only repository call to AdminFactory.getAdmin() is from JMSTestCase.setUp(), which loads properties from a classpath test resource selected by the test suite via the JVM property 'joram.jms.test.file'. Downstream modules include activemq-joram-jms-tests with Maven scope 'test' only. In practice this is a trusted extension/configuration point for local test execution, not a remotely reachable code-loading path. So the tool over-approximated the source by treating a test Properties parameter as user-controlled without tracing its concrete origin and usage context. This maps best to A1, not a real CWE-094 in deployment.
response.evidence: Dataflow in the report exists syntactically: AdminFactory.java lines 25-42 read props.getProperty('jms.provider.admin.class') and call Class.forName(adminClassName); admin = (Admin) adminClass.newInstance(). But the only caller in the repo is JMSTestCase.setUp() at lines 84-97, where props are loaded from getClass().getClassLoader().getResourceAsStream(System.getProperty('joram.jms.test.file', 'provider.properties')). The Joram suite sets that property itself: JoramJmsTest.java lines 71-83 call System.setProperty('joram.jms.test.file', 'provider.properties'). The corresponding test resource activemq-amqp/src/test/resources/provider.properties line 20 hardcodes jms.provider.admin.class=org.apache.activemq.transport.amqp.joram.ActiveMQAdmin. Maven metadata confirms test-only usage: activemq-amqp/pom.xml lines 72-78 and activemq-unit-tests/pom.xml lines 226-233 declare activemq-joram-jms-tests with <scope>test</scope>. Repository search found no non-test caller of AdminFactory.getAdmin(). Additionally, the loaded class must be on the local classpath and castable to org.objectweb.jtests.jms.admin.Admin, further showing this is an intentional test-plugin mechanism controlled by the test runner, not an attacker-controlled runtime injection path.

response.analysis: The reported dataflow is real, but it does not correspond to CWE-094. In `ActiveMQBlobMessage`, the remotely carried `remoteBlobUrl` is converted back to a `URL` by `getURL()`. In filesystem blob mode, `FileSystemBlobStrategy.getFile()` accepts any `file:` URL and returns `new File(message.getURL().toURI())`, and `getInputStream()` then opens `new FileInputStream(getFile(message))`. So a received blob message can influence which local file is opened if the client is configured to use the `file:` blob transfer strategy. However, this sink is only local file access; there is no code generation, code injection, script evaluation, class loading from this path, or any execution of attacker-supplied code. Therefore the report is a false positive for CWE-094. At most, this path suggests a different bug class such as arbitrary file read/delete in an opt-in local-filesystem blob deployment.
response.evidence: Relevant flow: `ActiveMQBlobMessageMarshaller.tightUnmarshal()` sets `remoteBlobUrl` from the wire format; `ActiveMQBlobMessage.getURL()` reconstructs it as `new URL(remoteBlobUrl)`; `ActiveMQMessageConsumer.createActiveMQMessage()` installs a `BlobDownloader` on received blob messages; `BlobTransferPolicy.createDownloadStrategy()` selects `FileSystemBlobStrategy` only when `getUploadUrl()` uses protocol `FILE`; then `FileSystemBlobStrategy.getInputStream()` calls `new FileInputStream(getFile(message))`, and `getFile()` does only a protocol check (`if !"file".contentEquals(protocol) throw`) before `return new File(message.getURL().toURI())`. This demonstrates attacker-influenced file selection, not code injection. There is no `Runtime.exec`, `ProcessBuilder`, script engine `eval`, dynamic compilation, or equivalent execution sink on this path. The report explanation itself says it is a 'path traversal/arbitrary file access pattern, not code injection', which confirms the mismatch with CWE-094.

response.analysis: The reported HTTP-body-to-`XStream.fromXML` flow is reachable, but the report's key claim is wrong for this codebase: this is not `fromXML` on an unrestricted XStream instance. `HttpTunnelServlet.doPost()` reads the request body and passes it to `wireFormat.unmarshalText(...)`, and `XStreamWireFormat.unmarshalText(Reader)` does call `getXStream().fromXML(reader)`. However, `XStreamWireFormat.createXStream()` builds that parser through `XStreamSupport.createXStream()`, which installs `NoTypePermission.NONE` and then a strict allowlist. By default, only primitives, arrays, `Collection`, `Map`, `String`, and classes under trusted package patterns are allowed; arbitrary attacker-chosen gadget classes are not. That missed hardening is the decisive reason this is a false positive. The `Command` cast in `HttpTunnelServlet` also means non-`Command` payloads do not reach broker command handling. In real deployments, this path may reject malformed/forbidden payloads or return an error for a request, but it is not a default CWE-094 code-execution issue. A materially different risk would exist only if an administrator explicitly disabled the restriction by setting `-Dorg.apache.activemq.SERIALIZABLE_PACKAGES=*`, which is an unsafe non-default override rather than the product's normal behavior.
response.evidence: Reachable path: `activemq-http/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/transport/http/HttpTunnelServlet.java:137-144` reads `request.getInputStream()` and executes `Command command = (Command) wireFormat.unmarshalText(new InputStreamReader(stream, "UTF-8"));`. `activemq-http/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/transport/xstream/XStreamWireFormat.java:69-70` then does `Object val = getXStream().fromXML(reader);`. Hardening that the report missed: `activemq-http/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/transport/xstream/XStreamWireFormat.java:125-127` creates XStream via `final XStream xstream = XStreamSupport.createXStream();`. `activemq-client/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/util/XStreamSupport.java:32-44` configures `stream.addPermission(NoTypePermission.NONE);`, adds only primitive/array/`Collection`/`Map`/`String` permissions, and allows wildcard types only for trusted packages. `activemq-client/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/util/ClassLoadingAwareObjectInputStream.java:42-43` sets the default trusted packages to `java.lang,org.apache.activemq,org.fusesource.hawtbuf,com.thoughtworks.xstream.mapper`, and `:92-93` shows `AnyTypePermission.ANY` is enabled only when the property is explicitly set to `*`. Post-deserialization constraint: `HttpTunnelServlet.java:144-165` casts the result to `Command` and only then calls `transport.doConsume(command)`; forbidden or non-`Command` objects fail before normal broker processing. The only `TransientInitializer` hooks on this path are benign field resets (`ConsumerInfo.initTransients`, `MessagePull.initTransients`, `ActiveMQObjectMessage.initTransients`), not execution primitives.
response.new_reason:: Secondary issue: the report is also misaligned with CWE-094. This code performs XML object deserialization, not code generation/evaluation; if the allowlist were absent, the issue would be closer to unsafe deserialization (for example CWE-502) than CWE-094.

response.analysis: The reported source is real and the control-flow path is plausibly reachable after a successful SAML login, especially for the IdP-initiated login branch. However, this is not a CWE-079 issue. The sink chosen by the tool is `setHeader("Location", redirectUri)`, which produces a 302 redirect and ends the response; it does not place attacker-controlled data into HTML, JavaScript, or any browser-executed page context. In other words, IRIS found a redirect/header flow, not an XSS sink. The code also treats `redirectTo` as an application redirect target by prepending a computed `baseUri`, so the feature is intended as a post-login redirect helper rather than page content generation. Therefore this report is a false positive for CWE-079 due to incorrect sink classification.
response.evidence: Source: `SamlUtil.getRedirectTo()` reads request-controlled input from `facade.getRequest().getQueryParamValue("redirectTo")` and, alternatively, from `facade.getRequest().getFirstParam(GeneralConstants.RELAY_STATE)` (`adapters/saml/core/src/main/java/org/keycloak/adapters/saml/SamlUtil.java:69-83`). Structure of the value: `buildRedirectTo()` constructs `baseUri + "/" + redirectTo` instead of rendering it into HTML (`SamlUtil.java:91-95`). Reachability: `ElytronSamlSessionStore.getRedirectUri()` only calls `SamlUtil.getRedirectTo(...)` when there is no saved original request (`adapters/saml/wildfly-elytron/src/main/java/org/keycloak/adapters/saml/elytron/ElytronSamlSessionStore.java:188-202`); in the normal SP-initiated flow, `AbstractInitiateLogin.challenge()` first saves the original request (`adapters/saml/core/src/main/java/org/keycloak/adapters/saml/AbstractInitiateLogin.java:65-67`). Sink: after authentication, the value is only used as `facade.getResponse().setHeader("Location", redirectUri); facade.getResponse().setStatus(302); facade.getResponse().end();` (`adapters/saml/core/src/main/java/org/keycloak/adapters/saml/profile/AbstractSamlAuthenticationHandler.java:497-501`). In the Elytron facade, `setHeader` delegates to `response.addResponseHeader(name, value)` (`adapters/saml/wildfly-elytron/src/main/java/org/keycloak/adapters/saml/elytron/ElytronHttpFacade.java:353-359`). There is no HTML response body or script/DOM sink in this path. Real use scenario: the adapter test data configures IdP-initiated SSO relay state as `redirectTo=/foo`, showing this parameter is intended as a post-login path redirect, not page content injection (`testsuite/integration-arquillian/tests/base/src/test/resources/adapter-test/keycloak-saml/testsaml.json:209-210`).
response.new_reason:: This path could merit separate review for non-XSS issues such as redirect semantics, but the report as filed does not establish CWE-079.

Explaination: User-controllable attributes from a public/provider-facing mapper are passed directly into FreeMarker template rendering; if the template outputs them in HTML, this can lead to XSS without sanitization.

response.analysis: Primary FP type is B1. The reported source is misidentified: `attributeMapper` is not attacker-controlled input, but an internal server-side SPI callback exposed through `LoginFormsProvider.setAttributeMapper(...)`. In the real codebase, the production uses of this mapper only rewrite existing model beans for organization/login behavior; they do not let an external user supply arbitrary HTML-bearing attributes. The sink is also overstated: passing a map into FreeMarker is not itself XSS in Keycloak, because `.ftl` templates are rendered with `HTMLOutputFormat`, and places that intentionally allow HTML use `kcSanitize(...)?no_esc`. So the report shows a generic map-to-template path, but not a concrete user-input-to-unsafe-HTML path that is exploitable in normal Keycloak deployments. Secondary FP aspects are shallow project-level reasoning (A1) and missed escaping/sanitization context (D1).
response.evidence: `FreeMarkerLoginFormsProvider.processTemplate` applies the mapper and forwards the result to FreeMarker at `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/forms/login/freemarker/FreeMarkerLoginFormsProvider.java:604-611`. However, the mapper API itself is only an internal Java SPI method declared at `server-spi-private/src/main/java/org/keycloak/forms/login/LoginFormsProvider.java:170`. The real production call sites are internal only, in `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/organization/authentication/authenticators/browser/OrganizationAuthenticator.java:252-259`, `341-360`, `405-413`, and `435-443`; these lambdas only wrap existing beans such as `AuthenticationContextBean`, `IdentityProviderBean`, and `RealmBean`, or set `USERNAME_HIDDEN`, rather than injecting raw request data into the template model. On the rendering side, `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/theme/freemarker/DefaultFreeMarkerProvider.java:56-63` sets `cfg.setOutputFormat(HTMLOutputFormat.INSTANCE)` for `.ftl` templates, and `:28-31` injects `kcSanitize` into the model. Templates explicitly sanitize raw-HTML outputs, for example `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/template.ftl:171` and `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/error.ftl:7`. Other outputs such as `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/info.ftl:7,11`, `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/login.ftl:15`, and `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/template.ftl:134` use normal `${...}` rendering under HTML output format, which is intended to be escaped rather than emitted as raw HTML. Therefore the report does not demonstrate a real external attacker-controlled XSS path in Keycloak core.

response.analysis: The reported source `JsonNode.asText()` can indeed carry attacker-controlled text, but the sink is not an HTML/JS rendering sink in Keycloak. This code lives in `integration/client-cli/admin-cli`, and the only reachable usage prints CSV output to stdout for a command-line user. CWE-079 requires untrusted data to be emitted into a browser-executed context such as HTML, a template, or DOM-generating output. That does not happen here. The report assumes that the generic `Consumer<String>` parameter could be an HTML renderer, but in the actual codebase no such consumer exists. So this is a false positive, primarily because the sink was misidentified (B1); the report also shows shallow project-context reasoning because it stops at a hypothetical sink instead of the real call site.
response.evidence: `OutputUtil.printAsCsv(object, fields, unquoted)` hardwires the sink to `IoUtil::printOut` at `integration/client-cli/admin-cli/src/main/java/org/keycloak/client/cli/util/OutputUtil.java:53-55`. `IoUtil.printOut` is just `System.out.println(msg)` at `integration/client-cli/admin-cli/src/main/java/org/keycloak/client/cli/util/IoUtil.java:162-164`, so the data is written to terminal/stdout, not to HTML. The only production call site is `printAsCsv(rootNode, returnFields, unquoted)` in `integration/client-cli/admin-cli/src/main/java/org/keycloak/client/admin/cli/commands/AbstractRequestCmd.java:374-379`, inside CLI response printing. The module entrypoint is `KcAdmMain.main(...)` at `integration/client-cli/admin-cli/src/main/java/org/keycloak/client/admin/cli/KcAdmMain.java:54-55`, confirming this is a command-line tool rather than a web endpoint. A repo-wide search finds no alternate invocation of the 4-argument `printAsCsv(..., Consumer<String>)` overload with any HTML-rendering consumer. Even if malicious text reaches `node.asText()` at `OutputUtil.java:103`, it is only printed as CLI text and will not execute as browser script.

response.analysis: The reported dataflow is largely real, but it does not produce CWE-079. `DefaultKeycloakContext.getUri()` wraps the request `UriInfo`, and `KeycloakUriInfo.getAbsolutePathBuilder()` reuses `delegate.getAbsolutePath()`, so request-derived URL components can reach the response `Location` header. However, the sink is `HttpHeaders.LOCATION` on a `201 Created` response, not HTML/JavaScript output. That cannot by itself trigger XSS, and this endpoint is not issuing a browser redirect status. So the core error is a wrong sink/CWE match (B1), with a secondary prompt/CWE deviation (C1) because the report itself describes a header-manipulation/open-redirect style issue rather than XSS. This is not an A1/A2 case: the path is reachable enough; the false positive comes from misclassifying the sink, not from an impossible path. It also does not cause a crash. Risk is further reduced because the appended path segment is a server-generated ID and the endpoint requires admin `manage-clients` permission.
response.evidence:
1) Request-derived source path exists: `uriInfo.put(type, new KeycloakUriInfo(session, type, getHttpRequest().getUri()));` in `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/DefaultKeycloakContext.java:93`. `KeycloakUriInfo` then does `absolutePath = delegate.getAbsolutePath();` and `return UriBuilder.fromUri(getAbsolutePath());` in `server-spi/src/main/java/org/keycloak/models/KeycloakUriInfo.java:83-92`.
2) The sink is only a response header, not page output: `response.setStatus(Response.Status.CREATED.getStatusCode());` and `response.addHeader(HttpHeaders.LOCATION, session.getContext().getUri().getAbsolutePathBuilder().path(clientInitialAccessModel.getId()).build().toString());` in `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/resources/admin/ClientInitialAccessResource.java:112-113`. `QuarkusHttpResponse.addHeader` just calls `addResponseHeader(name, value)` in `quarkus/runtime/src/main/java/org/keycloak/quarkus/runtime/integration/resteasy/QuarkusHttpResponse.java:54-55`.
3) The dynamic appended segment is server-generated, not attacker-controlled: `entity.setId(KeycloakModelUtils.generateId());` in `model/jpa/src/main/java/org/keycloak/models/jpa/RealmAdapter.java:2269-2270`.
4) The endpoint is admin-only: `auth.clients().requireManage();` in `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/resources/admin/ClientInitialAccessResource.java:88`.
5) Keycloak intentionally supports request/proxy-derived URL generation for `Location` in configured deployments, and tests show untrusted proxy headers are ignored unless enabled/trusted: see `quarkus/tests/integration/src/test/java/org/keycloak/it/cli/dist/ProxyHostnameV2DistTest.java:126-143`.

response.analysis: Step 1: the source is real because `Origin` is requester-controlled, but the selected sink is wrong for CWE-079. The reported sink is `response.setHeader("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", origin)`, which only writes an HTTP response header and does not place attacker data into HTML, JavaScript, or a browser-executable DOM context. Therefore this path does not satisfy the sink semantics of Cross-Site Scripting. Step 2: the dataflow itself is syntactically reachable, but for normal non-preflight requests Keycloak returns early unless the origin is in `allowedOrigins` or `*` is configured, so ordinary CORS responses are guarded by configured policy. Preflight paths can reflect the origin more broadly, but they are still only CORS header handlers. Step 3: A1/A2/D* are not the decisive cause here; the main error is the sink/CWE mismatch. Step 4: the report explanation itself admits this is 'not a Cross-Site Scripting sink', which is consistent with a false positive for CWE-079.
response.evidence: In `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/cors/DefaultCors.java` lines 136-150, Keycloak reads `String origin = request.getHttpHeaders().getRequestHeaders().getFirst(ORIGIN_HEADER);` and then writes `response.setHeader(ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_ORIGIN, origin);`. In `quarkus/runtime/src/main/java/org/keycloak/quarkus/runtime/integration/resteasy/QuarkusHttpResponse.java` lines 59-60, `setHeader` is implemented as `requestContext.serverResponse().setResponseHeader(name, value);`. That is response-header metadata, not an HTML/JS rendering sink. Also, `DefaultCors.java` lines 142-147 show a guard for non-preflight flows: if `allowedOrigins` is null or does not contain the request origin (and no wildcard is configured), the method returns without reflecting it. `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/protocol/oidc/utils/WebOriginsUtils.java` lines 34-44 show these allowed origins come from configured client web origins / resolved redirect URIs. `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/resources/account/CorsPreflightService.java` lines 19-23 shows reachable `preflight()` uses are `@OPTIONS` CORS handlers. So the concrete path is a CORS-header reflection path, not a browser script execution path; it does not establish CWE-079 and does not show a crash vector.

response.analysis: The reported flow is a real internal rendering flow, but it is not an exploitable CWE-079 in this component. The sink is the normal HTTP response writer, but the chosen source is wrong: `CssSkinExtensionPlugin.endParsing(String content, XWikiContext)` does not receive raw attacker input. In XWiki, `endParsing` is a plugin callback invoked after template/page rendering, and its `content` parameter is the already generated full response buffer. `Utils.parseTemplate()` creates `content` with `evaluateTemplate(...)`, runs placeholder replacement, then calls `getPluginManager().endParsing(content.trim(), context)`, and only then writes the final HTML to the response. `AbstractSkinExtensionPlugin.endParsing()` only replaces a fixed comment hook with generated import tags; it does not introduce a new untrusted source. In real deployment, if malicious HTML is already present in `content`, that issue would come from upstream page/template rendering or privileged scripting, not from this plugin callback. The plugin-added HTML is also constrained by authorization and escaping, so the report is a false positive caused by treating an internal response-buffer parameter as an external taint source.
response.evidence: 1. `XWikiPluginInterface.endParsing(...)` documents `content` as the full response to send to the client, showing it is an internal post-processing hook, not a request-input API. 2. `Utils.parseTemplate()` follows this order: `content = context.getWiki().evaluateTemplate(template + ".vm", context);` -> `content = replacePlaceholders(content, context);` -> `content = context.getWiki().getPluginManager().endParsing(content.trim(), context);` -> `response.getOutputStream().write(content.getBytes(...))` / `response.getWriter().write(content);`. 3. `XWikiPluginManager.endParsing()` only forwards that already rendered `content` through registered plugins. 4. `AbstractSkinExtensionPlugin.endParsing()` only executes `content.replaceFirst(hook, getImportString(context))` where `hook` is a fixed marker like `<!-- com.xpn... -->`. 5. The plugin-generated imports are guarded and escaped: `CssSkinExtensionPlugin.getLink()` uses `XMLUtils.escapeAttributeValue(...)`; `AbstractDocumentSkinExtensionPlugin.use()` calls `canResourceBeUsed(...)` which requires `Right.SCRIPT`; always-used extensions are limited to authors with `Right.ADMIN`; query parameters are URL-encoded by `sanitize(...)`.

Explaination: The source is a public method parameter and it flows into HTTP response output without escaping. Writing attacker-controlled HTML/content to the response can enable XSS.

response.analysis: The reported dataflow is real, but it is not a CWE-079 XSS vulnerability. Source identification is fine (`request.getParameter("language")` is user-controlled), and the control-flow path is reachable in `getDocLanguagePreferenceNew()`. However, the sink is `new Cookie(...)/addCookie(...)`, which creates a `Set-Cookie` HTTP header, not HTML/JS output. That means the report does not demonstrate attacker-controlled data reaching a browser-executable context, which is required for CWE-079. In addition, the request value is passed through `Util.normalizeLanguage()`, which canonicalizes locale-like values and maps many invalid inputs to `default`, further reducing direct exploitability on this path. So this is a false positive for CWE-079; at most it hints at a different class of issue (cookie/header handling), not XSS.
response.evidence: Reachable path: `XWikiServletRequest.getParameter()` returns the raw request parameter (`XWikiServletRequest.java:83-86`). `XWiki.getDocLanguagePreferenceNew()` then does `requestLanguage = Util.normalizeLanguage(context.getRequest().getParameter("language"));` (`XWiki.java:3354-3357`). If non-empty and not `default`, it stores that normalized value in a cookie: `language = requestLanguage; ... Cookie cookie = new Cookie("language", language); ... context.getResponse().addCookie(cookie);` (`XWiki.java:3390-3399`). Sanitization/canonicalization: `Util.normalizeLanguage()` lowercases, splits on `_-.'`, rebuilds a locale-like value, validates the language with `new Locale(...).getISO3Language()`, and returns `default` on invalid input (`Util.java:836-867`). Tests confirm examples such as `invalid code -> default`, `en -> en`, `DE_at -> de_AT` (`UtilTest.java:65-73`). Missing XSS sink: nowhere in the reported path is the value written into HTML, JavaScript, a template, or DOM context; the terminal operation is cookie/header creation only.

Explaination: User-controlled HTML/string content returned from a public plugin hook can flow to HTTP response writing without sanitization, enabling XSS.

response.analysis: The reported path is real, but it does not show attacker-controlled data reaching the response. `Utils.endParsing` sends the final rendered HTML to the HTTP response, and `XWikiPluginManager.endParsing()` lets registered server-side plugins post-process that HTML. That hook is an internal extension point, not an external input source. In XWiki, plugins are trusted Java classes loaded from configuration/classpath, so the report misclassifies a privileged plugin callback as a user-controlled source. In real deployments, a remote attacker cannot exploit this path unless they already control a plugin or have equivalent privileged script/admin capabilities, which is outside the CWE-79 boundary for the application itself. Therefore this is a false positive, primarily due to B1 (incorrect source selection).
response.evidence: Sink is real but expected: `Utils.java:180-184` computes the page content and calls `context.getWiki().getPluginManager().endParsing(content.trim(), context);`, then `Utils.java:215-217` writes that final `content` to the HTTP response. The source is not untrusted input: `XWikiPluginInterface.java:44` states a plugin must be registered in `xwiki.cfg`, and `XWikiPluginInterface.java:206-217` documents `endParsing` as a server-side post-processing hook over the full response. `XWiki.java:1649-1655` prepares plugins from wiki/config preferences, and `XWikiPluginManager.java:65-90` loads plugin classes via `Class.forName(...).getConstructor(...).newInstance(...)`; `XWikiPluginManager.java:268-276` then iterates over those plugin objects and calls `plugin.endParsing(...)`. This is trusted extension code, not request data. Built-in `endParsing()` implementations in this repo are also privileged and guarded: `AbstractDocumentSkinExtensionPlugin.java:223-225` only auto-loads always-used extensions when the author has `Right.ADMIN`; `AbstractDocumentSkinExtensionPlugin.java:248-250` and `316-317` require `Right.SCRIPT`; `AbstractDocumentSkinExtensionPlugin.java:452-456` checks `Right.VIEW`; and `JsSkinExtensionPlugin.java:91-93` XML-escapes the generated script URL attribute. If someone can install/modify such a plugin, or create script-enabled extensions, they already have privileged capabilities to inject active content by design, so this is not an exploitable XSS for an unprivileged attacker.

response.analysis: This report is a false positive for CWE-079. The dataflow to `MyFormAuthenticator.processLogin()` is real, but the sink is not an XSS sink: `response.sendRedirect(response.encodeRedirectURL(continueToURL))` performs an HTTP redirect by setting a `Location` header, not by emitting attacker-controlled HTML or JavaScript. `getContinueToURL()` returns either the request parameter `xredirect` or a saved-request URL from the session, so the code may represent an open-redirect pattern, but it does not satisfy the CWE-079 requirement of script/HTML injection and execution in a browser. The reported path is also somewhat imprecise: the `srid` / saved-request branch is session-bound and constrained to the same original URL, so the path through `SavedRequestRestorerFilter` is narrower than the tool suggests.
response.evidence: Relevant sink: `xwiki-platform-core/xwiki-platform-oldcore/src/main/java/com/xpn/xwiki/user/impl/xwiki/MyFormAuthenticator.java:248-249` -> `String continueToURL = getContinueToURL(request); response.sendRedirect(response.encodeRedirectURL(continueToURL));`. This is a redirect sink, not an HTML/JS rendering sink. Source handling: `MyFormAuthenticator.java:274-280` -> `String savedURL = request.getParameter("xredirect"); if (StringUtils.isEmpty(savedURL)) { savedURL = SavedRequestManager.getOriginalUrl(request); } if (!StringUtils.isEmpty(savedURL)) { return savedURL; }`. Saved-request branch: `SavedRequestManager.java:251-255` fetches `srid` from the request and returns `savedRequest.getRequestUrl() + "?srid=" + identifier`, where `savedRequest` comes from the current HTTP session. Restoration constraint: `SavedRequestRestorerFilter.java:255-260` only restores when `savedRequest != null && Strings.CS.equals(savedRequest.getRequestUrl(), request.getRequestURL().toString())`, then removes it from the session. No part of this path writes attacker-controlled data into an HTML page, a script block, a DOM sink, or a response body, so it cannot trigger CWE-079/XSS in the real world. At most, the direct `xredirect` branch suggests a different bug class: open redirect.

response.analysis: The report is a false positive. The reported sink is a generic AST-to-string serializer, but the reported source is not a real untrusted input source for CWE-79. RenderingScriptService.render(Block, String) only serializes an already-built Block tree; it does not read request data, does not execute transformations, and does not itself create dangerous HTML. In XWiki, an attacker would need to supply a specially crafted Block subtype such as an HTML RawBlock or use HTML/raw/script macros to get active markup into that Block tree. Those paths are separately protected by script-right checks and restricted-mode filtering. The report also shows no concrete caller path from external user input to this Block parameter, only a public-method parameter heuristic. So the finding depends on an overbroad source definition and missing project-level context, not on a reachable unprivileged XSS path.

HTMLMacroRequiredRightsAnalyzer.java:73-85 reports SCRIPT right for clean=false HTML macro usage and flags dangerous wiki/HTML content. Representative call sites also show non-attacker sources: rightpanels.vm:32-35 renders $panelUix.execute() after a view-right check, and editors/xdom_macros.vm:47-68 renders $edit.data but then escapes the result with $escapetool.xml inside a textarea. response.analysis: The reported data flow is real, but it does not constitute CWE-079. `req.getQueryString()` is user-controlled, and it is appended to a redirect target, but the sink is the HTTP `Location` header, not an HTML/JavaScript rendering sink. The code is implementing Jenkins' internal two-step authentication redirect (`/original` -> `/secured/original` -> `/original`). That may be relevant to redirect/header-handling reviews in general, but it is not reflected XSS by itself. The path is straightforward and reachable under specific authentication conditions, so this is not mainly an A1/A2 issue; the key problem is incorrect sink/vulnerability classification (B1). As a secondary observation, the report text itself admits this is 'not a direct XSS sink', which also shows misalignment with CWE-079.
response.evidence: In `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/BasicAuthenticationFilter.java:171-188`, Jenkins constructs `path = req.getContextPath() + "/secured" + path;`, then copies the query string with `String q = req.getQueryString(); if (q != null) path += '?' + q;`, and finally writes `rsp.setHeader("Location", path)`. In `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:4365-4370`, the `/secured` handler redirects back with `String path = req.getContextPath() + req.getOriginalRestOfPath();` plus the same query string, followed by `rsp.sendRedirect2(path)`. In both places, attacker input is only used as part of a URL for browser navigation; there is no HTML body output, no template rendering, no DOM/JS sink, and no server-side decoding of the query string into executable content. A payload such as `<script>alert(1)</script>` would remain part of the URL, not execute as script. The redirect target is also anchored to Jenkins paths via `req.getContextPath() + "/secured" + ...` / `req.getContextPath() + req.getOriginalRestOfPath()`, so the query string does not make this an attacker-chosen XSS target.

In the default Jenkins private security realm, usernames are also constrained by `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = ^[\\w-]+$`, which blocks CR/LF and markup characters needed for practical header-splitting or XSS payloads during normal local account creation. response.analysis: The reported flow is reachable, but it is not a CWE-079/XSS vulnerability. `AbstractAuthenticationToken.getName()` returns the principal/username (`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/AbstractAuthenticationToken.java:52-54`), `AuthenticationSpringImpl.getName()` delegates it (`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/AuthenticationSpringImpl.java:69-71`), and `ACL.checkPermission` throws `AccessDeniedException3` on permission failure (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/ACL.java:71-80`). The actual sink is `rsp.addHeader("X-You-Are-Authenticated-As", authentication.getName())` in `AccessDeniedException3` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:59-60`). That is an HTTP response header sink, not an HTML/JavaScript sink, so it does not satisfy CWE-079. This is therefore not an A2 unreachable-path case; the path is real. The decisive error is B1: incorrect sink/CWE selection. At most, if unsafe usernames with CR/LF were accepted, this would be a header-injection/response-splitting style issue rather than XSS. For default Jenkins accounts, the built-in `HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm` also restricts usernames with `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = "^[\\w-]+$"` and rejects invalid characters during signup, reducing even that alternate risk in the default realm.
response.evidence: Source/value derivation: `return principal instanceof UserDetails ? ((UserDetails) principal).getUsername() : String.valueOf(principal);` (`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/AbstractAuthenticationToken.java:52-54`). Reachability: `ACL.checkPermission` throws `new AccessDeniedException3(a, p)` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/ACL.java:76-80`), and `AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.handle` invokes `((AccessDeniedException3) cause).reportAsHeaders(rsp)` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.java:52-53`). Actual sink: `rsp.addHeader("X-You-Are-Authenticated-As", authentication.getName())` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:59-60`). Regression test confirms the value is exposed as an HTTP header, not page content: `assertEquals("user", x.getResponse().getResponseHeaderValue("X-You-Are-Authenticated-As"))` (`test/src/test/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3Test.java:97-100`). Built-in realm validation: `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = "^[\\w-]+$"` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm.java:123`) and signup rejects invalid usernames (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm.java:431-435`).

response.analysis: The reported dataflow is real and reachable, but it does not establish CWE-079. `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken.getName()` and `AuthenticationSpringImpl.getName()` are just delegates for the authenticated username, and `AccessDeniedException3.reportAsHeadersImpl` is invoked on real 403 paths. However, the reported sink is `HttpServletResponse.addHeader`, which places the value into an HTTP response header, not into an HTML/JavaScript execution context. That is not an XSS sink, so the report does not demonstrate CWE-079. The report explanation itself also says this would be "header injection ... not XSS", which further confirms the CWE mismatch. This is therefore a false positive for CWE-079, with the primary cause being incorrect sink/vulnerability-class selection rather than unreachable control flow.
response.evidence: Relevant sink in `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java`: `rsp.addHeader("X-You-Are-Authenticated-As", authentication.getName());` and optional group headers are also added via `addHeader(...)`. This method is reachable from `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.java` (`((AccessDeniedException3) cause).reportAsHeaders(rsp);`) and from `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonAuthenticationEntryPoint.java` (`cause.reportAsHeaders(rsp);`). Jenkins tests confirm this is a response-header path, not a body-rendering path: `test/src/test/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3Test.java` asserts `getResponseHeaderValue("X-You-Are-Authenticated-As")` equals `"user"`. In the same class there is a different method, `report(PrintWriter w)`, that writes `authentication.getName()` into the response body (`w.println("You are authenticated as: " + authentication.getName());`), and that method is separately marked with `@SuppressFBWarnings(value = "XSS_SERVLET", ...)`; but that is not the reported path. Also, Jenkins' built-in private realm restricts usernames during signup: `HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm.DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = "^[\\w-]+$"` and `validateAccountCreationForm` rejects usernames failing `containsOnlyAcceptableCharacters`, which further limits malicious built-in usernames, though that is secondary to the fact that the reported sink is not an XSS sink.

Vulnerability Path:
core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken.java:91-91: getName(...) : String
core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/AuthenticationSpringImpl.java:71-71: getName(...) : String
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:60-60: getName(...)

Explaination: User-controlled authentication name can flow into an HTTP response header via addHeader, enabling header injection if not sanitized; this is a relevant web vulnerability, not XSS.

Vulnerability Path:
core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:4366-4366: getQueryString(...) : String
core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:4370-4370: path

Explaination: User-controlled query string flows into an HTTP redirect target. This is not a direct XSS sink, but it is still a relevant web vulnerability pattern (open redirect/response header misuse) because untrusted input reaches sendRedirect2 without sanitization.

response.analysis: The source `req.getQueryString()` is user-controlled, but the selected sink is not a CWE-079 sink. In `Jenkins.doSecured`, Jenkins reconstructs the original Jenkins URL and issues an HTTP redirect after successful authentication; it does not render attacker-controlled data into HTML, JavaScript, or a browser-executable context. The surrounding project logic shows this is a fixed authentication bounce-back flow, not a free-form redirect chosen by the attacker. So even though untrusted data reaches a redirect target, this path does not by itself trigger XSS. Any real XSS would have to occur later in the destination page if that page separately reflected its parameters unsafely, which is outside the reported path. This is primarily B1 (wrong sink for CWE-079), and secondarily reflects shallow project-context reasoning: the report ignores that `doSecured` is only the return leg of the `/abc/def -> /secured/abc/def -> /abc/def` authentication flow. The report text itself also admits this is 'not a direct XSS sink' and shifts to open-redirect/header-misuse reasoning, which does not match CWE-079.
response.evidence: `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:4358-4370` shows the full behavior of the reported path: if authentication succeeded, Jenkins does `path = req.getContextPath() + req.getOriginalRestOfPath(); q = req.getQueryString(); if (q != null) path += '?' + q; rsp.sendRedirect2(path);`. This is a redirect response, not HTML/JS output. `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/BasicAuthenticationFilter.java:72-82` explicitly documents the intended control flow as `/abc/def -> /secured/abc/def -> /abc/def`, and `:171-177` constructs the first-leg redirect to `/secured...` with the same query string. Therefore `doSecured` is just sending the user back to the original local Jenkins URL after container auth, not to a new attacker-chosen script sink. Jenkins' own redirect-hardening logic (`core/src/main/java/hudson/Util.java:1653-1654`) defines unsafe redirects as absolute or scheme-relative targets, and `test/src/test/java/jenkins/security/Security3501Test.java:41-46` verifies those are rejected while local paths are allowed. The reported flow preserves the original query string on a same-origin return URL; it does not demonstrate script execution, browser crash, or a standalone exploitable CWE-079 issue.

B1 does not apply because the source and sink are correctly identified: the ZIP entry name is attacker-controlled input, and it is used to build a filesystem path that is passed to a real file-write sink. A1/A2 do not apply because this is not a speculative cross-procedural path; the data flow is direct inside the real import workflow. In `TaxonomyImportStep1.validateFormLogic()`, uploading a ZIP calls `importTaxonomyMediaFiles()`, which for matched entries calls `createTaxonomyLevelToImageMap()`. There, `fileName` is derived from `zipEntry.getName()` by stripping everything through `background/` or `teaser/`, but `../` segments are preserved. The code then constructs `new File(WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + "/" + fileName)` and writes to it with `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(...)` without canonicalization or a containment check. D1 also does not save this path: the only checks are ZIP readability and MIME-by-extension (`WebappHelper.getMimeType(fileName)`), neither of which sanitizes traversal. The write happens during step-1 validation, before later taxonomy APIs store the image normally, so the out-of-directory write already occurred even if the wizard is not completed. Reachability is privileged rather than public: the import UI is shown only when `secCallback.canImportExport()` is true. That limits exposure to taxonomy managers/admins, but it does not make the finding false.
response.evidence: Relevant flow in the real project code: (1) `TaxonomyTreeTableController.initForm()` adds the import/export action only if `secCallback.canImportExport()` is true, and `doOpenImportWizard()` opens `TaxonomyImportStep1` (`TaxonomyTreeTableController.java:179-180, 506`). (2) `TaxonomyImportStep1.validateFormLogic()` calls `importTaxonomyMediaFiles()` whenever a ZIP was uploaded (`TaxonomyImportStep1.java:407`). (3) In `importTaxonomyMediaFiles()`, the importer accepts entries matching `<prefix>media/.+`; then `finalZipEntry = zipEntry.getName().replaceAll(".*media", "").replaceAll("background/.*", "").replaceAll("teaser/.*", "")` is used only for taxonomy-level matching (`TaxonomyImportStep1.java:216-225`). A crafted entry like `xmedia/MATH/background/../../../../tmp/poc.png` yields `finalZipEntry = /MATH/`, so it can match an existing/imported taxonomy level while keeping the traversal payload after `background/`. (4) In `createTaxonomyLevelToImageMap()`, the same entry yields `fileName = zipEntry.getName().replaceAll(".+background/", "")`, i.e. `../../../../tmp/poc.png`; then `image = new File(WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + "/" + fileName)` and `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(in, image)` perform the write (`TaxonomyImportStep1.java:336-352`). (5) `WebappHelper.getTmpDir()` is just `System.getProperty("java.io.tmpdir")`, with no normalization (`WebappHelper.java:374`). (6) The later `storeBackgroundImage/storeTeaserImage` calls happen afterward in `TaxonomyTreeTableController.java:613-620`, so they do not prevent the initial path traversal write. - Medium: Recursive METS package references can escape the package directory at [AbstractPackageIngester.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/DSpace_codex/dspace-api/src/main/java/org/dspace/content/packager/AbstractPackageIngester.java:178) and [AbstractPackageIngester.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/DSpace_codex/dspace-api/src/main/java/org/dspace/content/packager/AbstractPackageIngester.java:296).
Child package references come from attacker-controlled METS content via `manifest.getChildMetsFilePaths()` in [AbstractMETSIngester.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/DSpace_codex/dspace-api/src/main/java/org/dspace/content/packager/AbstractMETSIngester.java:276) and are resolved with `new File(pkgFile.getParent(), childPkgRef)` without canonicalization or `startsWith()` enforcement. When recursive ingest/replace is used, a malicious package can reference `../` paths outside the package tree. This is not part of the default SWORD ingest path I reviewed; it is relevant to recursive package ingestion/replacement flows.

response.analysis: The report is a true positive, with limited exposure. The source and sink are correctly identified: child package references come from attacker-controlled METS `<mptr LOCTYPE="URL" xlink:href>` values via `METSManifest.getChildMetsFilePaths()`, and those values are later resolved in `AbstractPackageIngester.ingestAll()` / `replaceAll()` with `new File(pkgFile.getAbsoluteFile().getParent(), childPkgRef)`. The resulting `File` is then actually opened in the next recursive call by `AbstractMETSIngester.parsePackage()` using either `new FileInputStream(pkgFile)` or `new ZipFile(pkgFile)`. There is no canonicalization, no path-boundary check, and no sanitizer that keeps the child path under the parent package directory. This is not an A1/A2 false positive: the control flow is concrete and reachable when recursive ingest/replace is used. It is also not a B1 mistake, because the sink really is filesystem access outside the intended package tree. The main caveat is scope: default SWORD v1/v2 deposit paths do not enable recursive mode, so this is not exposed through the normal web deposit path. But it is reachable in real recursive AIP/METS ingest/replace workflows used by administrators, so it remains a real CWE-022 issue in that feature set.
response.evidence: `METSManifest.getChildMetsFilePaths()` returns raw child `href` strings without validation. `AbstractMETSIngester` adds them as package references only when `params.recursiveModeEnabled()` is true. `AbstractPackageIngester.ingestAll()` and `replaceAll()` then execute `File childPkg = new File(pkgFile.getAbsoluteFile().getParent(), childPkgRef);` and recursively call `ingestAll(context, null, childPkg, ...)` / `replaceAll(context, null, childPkg, ...)`. In the recursive callee, `AbstractMETSIngester.parsePackage()` opens that path with `new FileInputStream(pkgFile)` for manifest-only packages or `new ZipFile(pkgFile)` for external packages. Therefore a child reference such as `../outside.zip` or `../../other/mets.xml` escapes the package directory. The path is reachable through `org.dspace.app.packager.Packager`: option `-a` sets `pkgParams.setRecursiveModeEnabled(true)`, and the tool then calls `sip.ingestAll(...)` or `sip.replaceAll(...)`. By contrast, `SWORDMETSIngester` and `SwordMETSContentIngester` use fresh `PackageParameters` and call only `pi.ingest(...)` / `pi.replace(...)`, never enabling recursive mode. Practical impact is real but bounded: a malicious package supplied to an admin for recursive import/restore can force unintended filesystem access outside the package tree; if the target is not a valid package the import will fail/rollback, and if it is a valid local package it may be unintentionally ingested or used for replacement.

1. `External static file serving can traverse outside the configured directory`
Severity: High if external static files are enabled
Evidence:
- Requests hit static file handling in [MatcherFilter.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/http/matching/MatcherFilter.java:97) and [StaticFilesConfiguration.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:67).
- The external handler canonicalizes only `/`-based segments in [ExternalResourceHandler.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:66) via [UriPath.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:32).
- Later, [ExternalResource.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:40) calls `StringUtils.cleanPath`, which converts backslashes to `/` and resolves `..`.
- The final guard in [DirectoryTraversal.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:18) compares absolute path strings with raw `startsWith()` in [DirectoryTraversal.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:30), so `/var/www/public-private/secret.txt` is incorrectly treated as inside `/var/www/public`.
Why this is exploitable:
- If the app configures `staticFiles.externalLocation("/var/www/public")` and a sibling path like `/var/www/public-private/secret.txt` exists, a request for `GET /..%5Cpublic-private%5Csecret.txt` is transformed into `/var/www/public-private/secret.txt`.
- That path still passes the prefix check because `"/var/www/public-private/secret.txt".startsWith("/var/www/public")` is `true`.
- The file is then opened in [ExternalResource.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:66).

response.analysis: The report is a true positive. The source and sink are correctly identified: when an application enables `staticFiles.externalLocation(...)`, the HTTP request path flows into Spark's external static-file resolver and can reach a real file-read sink. This is not an unreachable or purely theoretical path. The key bug is that `ExternalResourceHandler` first applies `UriPath.canonical`, which only reasons about `/` separators, but `ExternalResource` later applies `StringUtils.cleanPath`, which converts `\\` to `/` and resolves `..`. That means a request such as `/..%5Cpublic-private%5Csecret.txt` can bypass the first normalization step, then be rewritten into a sibling path outside the configured directory. The final guard in `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal` is only a string-prefix check, so `/var/www/public-private/secret.txt` is incorrectly treated as being inside `/var/www/public`. If the target file exists and is readable, Spark will serve it. This is a real CWE-022 information-disclosure issue in the external static-files feature; it is configuration-dependent, but exploitable in realistic deployments.
response.evidence: Relevant runtime path: `StaticFilesConfiguration.consume()` -> `consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` -> `ExternalResource.getInputStream()`. In `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:66-83`, Spark canonicalizes the request path with `UriPath.canonical(path)`, concatenates it with the configured base via `addPaths(baseResource, path)`, constructs `new ExternalResource(addedPath)`, and only then checks `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)`. In `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-40`, the constructor does `new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path))`; `src/main/java/spark/utils/StringUtils.java:218-245` shows `cleanPath` replaces backslashes with `/` and resolves `..`. In `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:18-33`, the guard converts both strings to absolute paths and accepts them when `removeLeadingAndTrailingSlashesFrom(path).startsWith(removeLeadingAndTrailingSlashesFrom(folder))`. Therefore a normalized sibling path like `/var/www/public-private/secret.txt` passes the check for base `/var/www/public`. Finally, `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:66-67` opens the file with `new FileInputStream(file)`, and `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:91-97` serves it if readable. Reachability of `%5C`-based input is also supported by the project's own classpath traversal test: `src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java:141-145` expects a 400 for a `%5C`-encoded traversal request, which would not happen if the request form were impossible in practice. The external test at `src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-125` does not disprove the bug because it requests `/..%5C..%5Cspark%5CSpark.class`, which normalizes to `/spark/Spark.class`, not the sibling file the test created outside the static root.

3. `External static file serving also allows symlink-based escapes`
Severity: Medium
Evidence:
- [DirectoryTraversal.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/spark_codex/src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:26) uses `Paths.get(path).toAbsolutePath()`, not `toRealPath()`.
- That means symlink targets are not resolved before the boundary check.
Impact:
- If `/var/www/public/link -> /etc`, then `GET /link/passwd` is treated lexically as `/var/www/public/link/passwd` and passes the check, but the opened file is `/etc/passwd`.

response.analysis: This is a true positive. The source is the HTTP request path used for external static-file resolution, and the sink is the filesystem read performed when the matched resource is opened and copied to the response. The path is reachable in real deployments whenever an application enables `staticFiles.externalLocation(...)` / `externalStaticFileLocation(...)`. The code does normalize `.` and `..` segments with `UriPath.canonical`, so classic lexical traversal is filtered, but it does not resolve symlinks before enforcing the boundary. `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal` compares `Paths.get(path).toAbsolutePath()` against the configured base folder using a string prefix test, which is only lexical. `ExternalResource.getPath()` returns the unresolved path string, while `ExternalResource.getInputStream()` opens `new FileInputStream(file)`, which follows the symlink target at the OS/filesystem level. Therefore a request like `/link/passwd` under a base directory containing `link -> /etc` passes the check as `/base/link/passwd` but reads `/etc/passwd` in practice. This is not an A1/A2 false positive: the control flow is direct and runtime-reachable. It is not B1 either: the chosen source and sink are the correct ones for unauthorized file read. No sanitizer blocks the symlink case, so there is no D1-based false positive. Why this is exploitable:
- If the app configures `staticFiles.location("/public")` and its classpath contains a sibling resource such as `public-jar.zip` or `public-private/...`, a request like `GET /..%5Cpublic-jar.zip` resolves to `public-jar.zip`.

response.analysis: This is a true positive. The report’s source and sink are correctly identified: user-controlled request path data flows from `HttpServletRequest.getServletPath()/getPathInfo()` in `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(...)` into classpath resource resolution in `ClassPathResourceHandler`, then into `ClassLoader.getResource()/getResourceAsStream()`, and finally out to the HTTP response. The bug is real because Spark first canonicalizes using `UriPath.canonical()`, which only understands `/`, so a backslash traversal such as `..\` survives. Later, `ClassPathResource` calls `StringUtils.cleanPath()`, which converts `\` to `/` and collapses `..`, turning `/public/..\public-jar.zip` into `public-jar.zip`. The final boundary check in `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstInClassPath()` is only a raw prefix test, so any sibling classpath resource whose name starts with the configured root string (for example `public-jar.zip` or `public-private/...`) is incorrectly accepted. This is not an A1/A2/B1/C1-style false positive: the path is reachable in the normal static-file serving flow, the source/sink are correct, and the only sanitizer is incomplete rather than absent from the analysis.
response.evidence: Concrete dataflow: `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` builds `pathInContext` from the request (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59`), then `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource(String)` canonicalizes it, prepends the configured base, constructs a `ClassPathResource`, and calls the traversal guard (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:61-84`). `UriPath.canonical()` only searches for `/` separators, so `..\\` is not normalized away there (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:37-57, 69-149`). `ClassPathResource` then validates and normalizes with `StringUtils.cleanPath()`, which explicitly replaces backslashes with slashes and resolves `..` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResource.java:76-87, 94-110`; `src/main/java/spark/utils/StringUtils.java:218-267`). The classpath boundary check is only `return rlatsPath.startsWith(rlatsFolder);` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:30-33`), so `public-jar.zip` passes against base `/public`. If it exists, Spark serves it: `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` accepts any readable resource and copies its stream to the response (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:85-105`), while `ClassPathResource.exists()` / `getInputStream()` resolve through the classloader (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResource.java:143-172`) and `AbstractFileResolvingResource.isReadable()` returns true for non-file classpath URLs as well (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractFileResolvingResource.java:105-119`). Reachability is supported by the project’s own integration test: `StaticFilesTest` configures `staticFiles.location("/public")` and sends an encoded backslash traversal payload, expecting Spark to process it as traversal (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java:77-78, 141-146`). That proves the `%5C` request form reaches the static-file logic in the default embedded-server setup. The repository also already contains a sibling root resource named `src/test/resources/public-jar.zip`, which matches the report’s example and illustrates the practical impact.
