2. One-shot queries on caller-managed connections retain `PreparedStatement`s until the entire connection is closed.
`buildPreparedStatement()` caches and registers each statement with the connection. After that, `executeUpdate()`, `executeBatch()`, `executeAndFetch*()`, and `executeAndFetchTable*()` do not call `Query.close()`; they only call `closeConnectionIfNecessary()`, which is a no-op for `Sql2o.open()` and `beginTransaction()` connections because `autoClose` is `false`. `ResultSetIterableBase.close()` only closes the `ResultSet`, not the owning `PreparedStatement`, so even callers who correctly close lazy iterables do not fully release the JDBC resources for that query. In long-lived transactions or external transactions, this can accumulate open statements/cursors until the database refuses more work.
Refs: [Query.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:423), [Query.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:435), [Query.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:477), [Query.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:498), [Query.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:670), [Query.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:862), [Query.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:926), [Sql2o.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Sql2o.java:197), [Sql2o.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Sql2o.java:205), [Sql2o.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Sql2o.java:293), [Connection.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:267), [Connection.java](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/sql2o_codex/core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:286)

response.analysis: This is a true positive CWE-772. `Query.buildPreparedStatement()` creates a `PreparedStatement`, stores it in the `Query`, and registers it in `Connection.statements` (`core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:423-435`). On caller-managed sql2o connections, `Sql2o.open()` and `beginTransaction()` create `Connection(..., false)`, so `autoClose` is false (`core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Sql2o.java:197-206, 293-309`). The successful paths of `executeUpdate()` and `executeBatch()` never call `Query.close()`; they only call `closeConnectionIfNecessary()`, which is a no-op when `autoClose` is false (`Query.java:670-685, 862-881, 926-930`). The materializing fetch helpers (`executeAndFetch*`, `executeAndFetchTable*`, and therefore `executeScalarList`) route through `ResultSetIterableBase.close()`, which closes only the `ResultSet` and again does not close the owning `PreparedStatement` (`Query.java:457-503, 612-666`). The first general release point for those statements is later `Connection.close()`, which iterates the tracked `statements` set and closes them (`core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:267-293`). This is a direct, value-independent path, so it is not an A1/A2-style false positive. One nuance is that `executeScalar()` uses try-with-resources for its statement (`Query.java:700-718`), so that method itself is narrower and not the reported leak path.
response.evidence: Concrete path for `try (Connection con = sql2o.open()) { con.createQuery(sql).executeUpdate(); ... }`: (1) `open()` returns `new Connection(this, false)`; (2) `createQuery()` returns a new `Query`; (3) `executeUpdate()` calls `buildPreparedStatement()`; (4) `buildPreparedStatement()` runs `jdbcConnection.prepareStatement(...)` and `connection.registerStatement(preparedStatement)`; (5) after execution, the `finally` block calls only `closeConnectionIfNecessary()`, and because `autoClose == false` nothing is closed; (6) the statement remains live in `Connection.statements` until outer `Connection.close()`. For fetches, `executeAndFetch(factory)` uses `try (ResultSetIterable<T> iterable = executeAndFetchLazy(factory))`, but `ResultSetIterableBase.close()` only does `rs.close()` before the same no-op `closeConnectionIfNecessary()`. `NoQuirks.closeStatement()` is just `statement.close()` (`core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/quirks/NoQuirks.java:154-156`), so closing the `ResultSet` is not an equivalent release path. README and tests commonly use one `Connection con = sql2o.open()` for multiple queries without closing each `Query`, so in long-lived transactions, callbacks, or externally managed connection scopes, repeated one-shot queries can accumulate open statements/cursors until the database refuses more work, causing availability/DoS issues.

3. Generated-key `ResultSet`s are drained but never explicitly closed.
`executeUpdate()` and `executeBatch()` call `statement.getGeneratedKeys()` and pass the returned `ResultSet` into `Connection.setKeys()`. `setKeys()` copies the values into memory, but it does not close the `ResultSet` afterward. That leaves the generated-key cursor alive for as long as the owning statement stays open, which amplifies the statement-retention issue above.

response.analysis: The report is a true positive for CWE-772. The source/sink are correctly identified: `Query.executeUpdate()` and `Query.executeBatch()` obtain a generated-keys `ResultSet` from `PreparedStatement.getGeneratedKeys()` and pass it into `Connection.setKeys()`, which drains it into memory but never closes it. This is reachable in normal code paths, not a theoretical path: `Connection.createQuery(String)` enables generated keys by default via `NoQuirks.returnGeneratedKeysByDefault() == true`, and the recommended/project-common usage is `Connection con = sql2o.open()` or transaction APIs where `autoClose` is false. In those paths, `closeConnectionIfNecessary()` is a no-op, so the owning `PreparedStatement` remains registered and open until `Query.close()` or `Connection.close()`/transaction end. Because the generated keys are fully copied in `setKeys()`, the `ResultSet`'s effective lifetime ends immediately there, but the cursor/resource is retained for the rest of the statement/connection lifetime. That is exactly a missing release after effective lifetime. This is not a B1 mistake, not an A1/A2 unreachable-path issue, and not a D1 false alarm: later statement/connection closure is only an eventual cleanup, not an immediate release that negates the finding. The impact is mainly resource-exhaustion/DoS in long-lived connections or transactions that perform many inserts/updates, where many statements and generated-key cursors can accumulate.
response.evidence: Reachable path: `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:670-685` calls `PreparedStatement statement = buildPreparedStatement();`, then `statement.executeUpdate();`, then `this.connection.setKeys(this.returnGeneratedKeys ? statement.getGeneratedKeys() : null);`. The sink does not close the `ResultSet`: `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:191-202` iterates `while(rs.next())` and stores values, but has no `rs.close()`. The statement is intentionally retained: `buildPreparedStatement()` registers it in `Connection.statements` at `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:423-435`; it is only closed by `Query.close()` at `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:398-406` or by `Connection.close()` iterating the registered statements at `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:267-293`. On common non-auto-close usage, `closeConnectionIfNecessary()` does nothing because it only closes when `connection.autoClose` is true (`core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Query.java:926-931`), while `Sql2o.open()` creates `Connection(this, false)` (`core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Sql2o.java:205-206`). 1. Medium: rejected `ExecutorScheduler` worker submissions can leak task wrappers and anything they capture. In the worker path, the code adds the task to the worker’s `CompositeDisposable` before attempting submission; if the executor rejects, it sets `disposed = true` but does not dispose or delete the already-tracked task. Later `dispose()` becomes a no-op because it is guarded by `if (!disposed)`, so the rejected task stays retained. This is true for delayed scheduling in all modes, and for immediate scheduling when `interruptibleWorker` is enabled. Fair mode also has an unhandled resubmission rejection path that can strand queued work. Repeated rejections from a bounded or shutting-down executor can therefore cause unbounded memory growth. response.analysis: The report is a true positive. The source/sink pairing is correct for CWE-772: user-supplied `Runnable`s are wrapped and added to worker-owned tracking structures, and on rejection those wrappers are not released. In `ExecutorScheduler.ExecutorWorker.schedule(Runnable)`, when `interruptibleWorker` is enabled, the task is added to `tasks` before submission (`ExecutorScheduler.java:162-165`), but if `executor.execute(this)` throws, the catch block only sets `disposed = true`, clears the queue, and returns (`ExecutorScheduler.java:177-184`). It never disposes or deletes the already-added `InterruptibleRunnable`. In delayed scheduling, `ScheduledRunnable sr` is added to `tasks` first (`ExecutorScheduler.java:207-208`), and if `ScheduledExecutorService.schedule(...)` rejects, the catch block again only sets `disposed = true` and returns (`ExecutorScheduler.java:210-217`). This is not a theoretical path: RxJava's own API docs explicitly discuss `RejectedExecutionException` for shut-down or bounded executors (`Schedulers.java:366-374`, `444-452`, `528-536`), and tests exercise these rejection paths (`ExecutorSchedulerTest.java:287-305`, `ExecutorSchedulerInterruptibleTest.java:201-219`, `ExecutorSchedulerFairTest.java:204-222`). There is also no sanitizer after rejection: `CompositeDisposable.add()` only auto-disposes when the container is already disposed, not when disposal happens later (`CompositeDisposable.java:98-114`); `InterruptibleRunnable` removes itself only from `run()`/`dispose()` (`ExecutorScheduler.java:389-449`); `ScheduledRunnable` removes itself only from `run()`/`dispose()` (`ScheduledRunnable.java:76-148`). After rejection, neither callback runs, and later `ExecutorWorker.dispose()` is a no-op because it is guarded by `if (!disposed)` (`ExecutorScheduler.java:229-237`). Real-world reachability exists because many operators retain the worker after a failed schedule. For example, `FlowableSubscribeOn.subscribeActual` stores the worker in `SubscribeOnSubscriber` and then calls `w.schedule(sos)` while ignoring the returned `Disposable` (`FlowableSubscribeOn.java:43-49`, `51-75`); if scheduling is rejected, the downstream still holds `SubscribeOnSubscriber`, which still holds `worker`, which still holds leaked task wrappers/captured objects. Fair mode has an additional real bug: `runFair()` resubmits via `executor.execute(this)` without a rejection handler (`ExecutorScheduler.java:254-271`), so a shutdown-time rejection can strand queued tasks and their captures.
response.evidence: Immediate interruptible worker path: `tasks.add(interruptibleTask)` at `ExecutorScheduler.java:163-164`; rejection catch at `177-184` does not call `tasks.delete(...)` or `tasks.dispose()`. Delayed worker path: `tasks.add(sr)` at `207-208`; rejection catch at `214-217` also does not release `sr`. Cleanup logic exists only inside `InterruptibleRunnable.dispose()/run()` (`ExecutorScheduler.java:420-449`) and `ScheduledRunnable.dispose()/run()` (`ScheduledRunnable.java:76-148`), so a never-submitted rejected task stays in `CompositeDisposable`. `ExecutorWorker.dispose()` cannot fix this afterwards because once the catch sets `disposed = true`, the later `dispose()` body is skipped by `if (!disposed)` (`ExecutorScheduler.java:230-237`). Reachability is practical, not hypothetical: `FlowableSubscribeOn` retains the `Worker` in `SubscribeOnSubscriber.worker` before attempting `w.schedule(sos)` (`FlowableSubscribeOn.java:44-49`, `56-75`). If rejection occurs, no normal completion path disposes the worker, and the retained worker keeps the leaked wrappers. The project's own tests show the rejection scenarios are expected to occur with a shut-down executor (`ExecutorSchedulerTest.java:287-305`, `ExecutorSchedulerInterruptibleTest.java:201-219`, `ExecutorSchedulerFairTest.java:204-222`).

response.analysis: The reported behavior is a real API/lifecycle sharp edge, but it is not a true CWE-772 vulnerability in RxJava itself. The report treats `SchedulerWhen.dispose()` as if it owned and had to release all `Worker` instances, but RxJava's contract assigns that responsibility to the caller that obtained the `Worker`. In normal use, the library expects either the raw `Worker` or the returned `Disposable` from scheduling to be disposed by its owner; continuing to create/use workers after the scheduler wrapper has been disposed is misuse of a disposed object. The post-disposal queueing path is therefore caller-induced, not an intrinsic library-side missing-release bug. In addition, for intended combinator chains, disposing the top-level subscription cancels `workerProcessor`, so the top-level processor does not keep accumulating new workers after disposal.
response.evidence: `Scheduler.java:166-171` explicitly says the caller must release each created `Worker` via `Worker.dispose()` to avoid leaks. `SchedulerWhen.java:287-294` shows the actual release point: `QueueWorker.dispose()` completes `actionProcessor` and disposes `actualWorker`. `SchedulerWhen.java:120-122` only disposes the combinator subscription (`disposable.dispose()`); it is not the owner of previously created workers. For proper combinator chains, cancellation propagates to `workerProcessor`, and `UnicastProcessor.onNext()` becomes a no-op when `done || cancelled` (`UnicastProcessor.java:436-444`), with cancellation clearing queued items (`UnicastProcessor.java:531-544`). So the library does not itself forget to release a resource it owns; the problematic state only arises if application code keeps using a disposed `SchedulerWhen` and/or fails to dispose the worker or scheduling `Disposable` it created.

2. Low: `SchedulerWhen.dispose()` does not fully end worker lifetime, and post-disposal worker creation can retain queued actions indefinitely. The top-level `dispose()` only cancels the combinator subscription; it does not dispose already-created `QueueWorker`s, and `createWorker()` does not check whether the wrapper is already disposed. After disposal, new workers still allocate a backing `actualWorker` plus an `actionProcessor`, but their `actions` stream is no longer consumed; later `schedule()` calls keep pushing `ScheduledAction` objects into an unbounded `UnicastProcessor` queue, and only manual `QueueWorker.dispose()` releases the backing worker. That is a missing-release condition after the scheduler’s effective lifetime ends. Refs: [SchedulerWhen.java:120](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/RxJava_codex/src/main/java/io/reactivex/rxjava3/internal/schedulers/SchedulerWhen.java:120), [SchedulerWhen.java:132](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/RxJava_codex/src/main/java/io/reactivex/rxjava3/internal/schedulers/SchedulerWhen.java:132), [SchedulerWhen.java:287](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/RxJava_codex/src/main/java/io/reactivex/rxjava3/internal/schedulers/SchedulerWhen.java:287), [SchedulerWhen.java:304](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/RxJava_codex/src/main/java/io/reactivex/rxjava3/internal/schedulers/SchedulerWhen.java:304), [UnicastProcessor.java:436](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/RxJava_codex/src/main/java/io/reactivex/rxjava3/processors/UnicastProcessor.java:436).

`[DataUtil.streamParser(Path...)](</data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/jsoup_codex/src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/DataUtil.java:139>)` (`139-149`) and `[HttpConnection.Response.streamParser](</data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/jsoup_codex/src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:1012>)` (`1012-1026`) have the same problem for progressive parsing: if charset detection or the initial `StreamParser.parse()` fails before the parser is returned, the file/socket body is not closed. response.analysis: This is a true positive CWE-772 report. The reported source and sink are correctly identified: untrusted file/stream/HTTP response input reaches parser setup code that can throw before resource ownership is transferred, and the underlying file/socket stream is not reliably closed on those exceptional paths. This is not an A1/A2 false positive because the path is concrete and runtime-reachable, not a hypothetical control-flow construction. It is also not B1, C1, or a D-series misread: there is no hidden sanitizer or alternate cleanup on the failing path.
response.evidence: In `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/DataUtil.java:223-233`, `parseInputStream(...)` closes the input only if `charsetDoc != null` in the `finally` block. If `detectCharset(...)` throws before returning, `charsetDoc` stays null and the input is left open. That path is real: `detectCharset(...)` disables underlying close with `input.allowClose(false)` at `DataUtil.java:251-260`, then parses through a `try (Reader reader = new SimpleStreamReader(...))`. But `ControllableInputStream.close()` is a no-op while `allowClose` is false (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/internal/ControllableInputStream.java:181-187`), so the try-with-resources only closes the wrapper, not the actual stream. The failure itself is reachable because `TreeBuilder.initialiseParse()` constructs `new CharacterReader(input)` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/parser/TreeBuilder.java:43-52`), and `CharacterReader` immediately calls `bufferUp()` in its constructor (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/parser/CharacterReader.java:51-57`); `doBufferUp()` wraps reader I/O failures as `UncheckedIOException` (`CharacterReader.java:104-117`). For progressive parsing, `DataUtil.streamParser(Path...)` (`DataUtil.java:139-149`) opens the file first, then calls `detectCharset(openStream(path), ...)`; if that throws, `streamer.close()` does not close the file because the `StreamParser` does not yet own the stream. Also, if `streamer.parse(reader, ...)` throws `UncheckedIOException`, its `catch(IOException)` does not run. `HttpConnection.Response.streamParser()` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:1012-1026`) has no cleanup around `detectCharset(...)` or `streamer.parse(...)`; if either fails, `safeClose()` is skipped and the HTTP body stream / socket cleanup path is not executed. `HttpConnection.Response.parse()` (`HttpConnection.java:1003-1008`) also reaches `DataUtil.parseInputStream(...)`, so it inherits the same setup-time leak if `detectCharset(...)` fails early. One nuance: unsupported charset values discovered from HTML meta tags are sanitized by `validateCharset()` (`DataUtil.java:369-380`), so that specific trigger is overstated in the report; however, I/O failures and explicit invalid `charsetName` values still produce the leak. In real use, jsoup commonly parses attacker-controlled remote responses or uploaded files, so repeated malformed/truncated inputs can accumulate open file descriptors or sockets and cause DoS.

response.analysis: The report is a true positive. The source is correctly identified: realm localization text can be written through the admin localization API with no sanitization or key allowlist, and those values are intentionally merged into the login theme message bundle. The sink is also correct: several login/WebAuthn templates place `msg(...)` directly into inline JavaScript string/object literals and force raw output with `?no_esc`; `MessageFormatterMethod` formats the text but does not perform JavaScript escaping. There is no compensating sanitizer such as `?js_string`, JSON encoding, or `kcSanitize` on these JS sinks. The path is reachable in real use because the affected templates are used by normal WebAuthn/passkey login or registration flows, and Keycloak's default CSP does not define `script-src`, so these inline module scripts are allowed to run. One nuance is that the report's exact sample payload may need small adjustment because Java `MessageFormat` treats some pattern characters specially, but exploitable payloads that only rely on raw double quotes/commas/colons still work. This is therefore a stored admin-to-user XSS, not an A1/A2/B1/D1/C1 false positive.
response.evidence: Source: `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/resources/admin/RealmLocalizationResource.java:84-89` stores arbitrary `text` via `session.realms().saveLocalizationText(...)`, and the bulk endpoints at `:110-139` accept arbitrary `Map<String,String>` values after only `requireManageRealm()`. Merge: `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/forms/login/freemarker/FreeMarkerLoginFormsProvider.java:422-426` loads `theme.getEnhancedMessages(realm, locale)` and exposes it as `msg`; `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/util/LocaleUtil.java:196-200` shows realm localization texts are merged over theme messages. Real login-page use is confirmed by `testsuite/integration-arquillian/tests/base/src/test/java/org/keycloak/testsuite/i18n/LoginPageTest.java:376-400`, which saves a realm localization entry and asserts the modified text appears on the login page. Reachability: `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/authentication/requiredactions/WebAuthnRegister.java:176` renders `webauthn-register.ftl`; `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/authentication/authenticators/browser/PasskeysConditionalUIAuthenticator.java:39-41` renders `login-passkeys-conditional-authenticate.ftl`; `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/login.ftl:1-2,83` imports and invokes the `passkeys.ftl` macro. response.analysis: This is a real stored XSS, although the report overstates one part of the write path. The sink is genuine: `select-organization.ftl` renders `organization.alias` inside an inline JavaScript string in `onclick`. The alias reaches that template through `OrganizationAuthenticator.shouldUserSelectOrganization()` -> `ProfileBean.getOrganizations()` -> `OrganizationBean.getAlias()` with no JavaScript-context escaping. Keycloak configures FreeMarker for HTML output, but that only gives HTML escaping; it does not apply `?js_string`-style escaping for a JavaScript string literal. That matters because HTML entities such as `'` are decoded by the browser before the inline event handler is executed, so a quote in the alias can still break out of the JS string. The create path is sufficient to store such a payload: `OrganizationsResource.create()` only calls `ReservedCharValidator.validateNoSpace()`, and that validator does not reject quotes or backticks. Even with `(`, `)`, `;`, `/`, and spaces blocked, code execution is still possible because allowed characters such as `'`, `` ` ``, and `-` are enough to build valid JavaScript in this handler context. The report is inaccurate about the update path, because aliases are effectively immutable after creation, but that does not invalidate the vulnerable create-to-render path. Reachability is conditional, not theoretical: the page is shown when a user belongs to multiple organizations and the organization scope resolves to `ANY`, and the victim is expected to click one of the listed organizations, which triggers the malicious handler. Default login-page CSP does not neutralize this, because Keycloak's default CSP does not define `script-src`, so inline event handlers are still allowed. The source requires realm-management privileges, so this is a privileged stored-XSS vector rather than a low-privilege one, but it is still a true CWE-79 issue.

File:
- [extension.vm](/data/lifengjie/LLM4Security/data/codex_java_projects/xwiki-platform_codex/xwiki-platform-core/xwiki-platform-web/xwiki-platform-web-templates/src/main/resources/templates/extension.vm:448)
Relevant lines:
- `href="$extension.webSite"`
- `href="$extension.repository.descriptor.URI"`
- `href="$extension.scm.url"`
- `href="$extension.scm.connection.path"`
- `href="$extension.issueManagement.getURL()"`
Why this is risky:
- These values are inserted into `href` attributes without a URL sanitizer.
- Visible label text is escaped, but the link target is not validated against dangerous schemes.
- If extension metadata can be controlled by a malicious extension package or repository entry, values like `javascript:alert(1)` or crafted URLs would become clickable active content.
Confidence:
- High that this is unsafe coding.
- Slightly lower than finding 1 because exploitability depends on whether attacker-controlled extension metadata can reach this view in the target deployment model.

response.analysis: The report is a true positive. In `extension.vm`, XWiki renders extension metadata directly into active `href` attributes (`$extension.webSite`, `$extension.scm.url`, `$extension.issueManagement.getURL()`, etc.) without URL validation and without even XML-escaping the attribute value. The source is real: `AbstractExtensionRESTResource.createExtension()` copies `PROP_EXTENSION_WEBSITE`, `PROP_EXTENSION_SCMURL`, and `PROP_EXTENSION_ISSUEMANAGEMENT_URL` from the extension store into the extension object as raw strings, and `ExtensionIndexStore.add()` indexes `extension.getWebSite()` unchanged. This is not shallow or unreachable reasoning: `ExtensionIT.testShowDetails()` builds a remote `TestExtension`, sets website/SCM/issue-management metadata, adds it to the repository, opens the Extension Manager UI, and asserts that the rendered `href` equals the original metadata value. XWiki also has an intended defense path that is skipped here: `macros.vm` defines `#getSanitizedURLAttributeValue()` using `services.security.url.parseToSafeURI()` and `services.html.isAttributeSafe()`, and `MacrosTest` shows `javascript:alert('foobar')` is rejected by that safe path. Therefore the source/sink pair is correct, the path is reachable, no sanitizer blocks it, and the finding matches CWE-079 as a click-triggered stored XSS / unsafe URL injection. Exploitation requires attacker-controlled extension metadata and a victim clicking the rendered link, so the issue is not an automatic page-load XSS, but it is still a real vulnerability. `repository.descriptor.URI` and `scm.connection.path` are somewhat less certain because their controllability depends on repository / SCM parsing semantics, but the report as a whole remains true because multiple clearly attacker-controlled string fields reach the sink unsanitized.

Source population: `xwiki-platform-repository-server-api/.../AbstractExtensionRESTResource.java:349-387` does `extension.setWebsite((String) ...)`, `scm.setUrl((String) ...)`, and `issueManagement.setUrl((String) ...)` from stored metadata with no scheme check. Index/storage path: `xwiki-platform-extension-index/.../ExtensionIndexStore.java:588-593` stores `extension.getWebSite()` unchanged. Reachability proof: `xwiki-platform-extension-test-tests/.../ExtensionIT.java:355-391` sets `extension.setWebsite("http://www.alice.com")`, `extension.setScm(...)`, `extension.setIssueManagement(...)`, then asserts `webSiteLink.getAttribute("href") == extension.getWebSite() + '/'`, proving metadata -> rendered link end to end. Existing sanitizer not used: `xwiki-platform-web-templates/.../macros.vm:3234-3244` defines `#getSanitizedURLAttributeValue()` with `services.security.url.parseToSafeURI()` + `services.html.isAttributeSafe()`, and `xwiki-platform-web-templates/.../MacrosTest.java:347-385` verifies `javascript:alert('foobar')` is rejected. Same-template contrast: `extension.vm:1022-1031` checks supporter URLs with `services.html.isAttributeSafe('a', 'href', $extensionSupporterPlan.uRL)` before rendering, showing these metadata links are missing an available protection.

response.analysis: The report is a true positive. The source is correctly identified: `MessageSenderMacro.xml` reads `request.messagestream_message`, trims it, and for authenticated users passes it unchanged to `services.messageStream.postPublicMessage(...)`, `postPersonalMessage(...)`, `postMessageToGroup(...)`, or `postDirectMessageToUser(...)`. In `DefaultMessageStream.createMessageEvent()`, the only processing is `StringUtils.left(message, 2000)`; the body is stored with `e.setBody(...)` and there is no HTML escaping or sanitization. The sink is also correctly identified and is reachable: the legacy message-stream notification templates for `publicMessage`, `personalMessage`, `directMessage`, and `groupMessage` call macros that output `$messageEvent.body` directly inside `<blockquote>` in both the web notification template and the HTML email template. This is not a shallow or theoretically unreachable path: these event types have concrete templates and the notification renderer executes them for matching events. No sanitizer is applied on this path. XWiki's template engine also does not auto-escape this output here: `TemplateManager.execute()` runs the Velocity template, and `InternalTemplateManager` / `TemplateAsyncRenderer` wrap the evaluated result as a `RawBlock` in the target syntax; the notification UI then renders that block to `html/5.0`. So attacker-controlled HTML is preserved as HTML, not converted to safe text. This matches CWE-079. In real use, this is authenticated stored XSS in the web notification UI. The HTML email sink is more client-dependent, but the web sink alone is sufficient to classify the issue as a real security vulnerability.
response.evidence:
Data flow: `request.messagestream_message` -> `MessageSenderMacro.xml` posts `$message` through `services.messageStream.post*Message(...)` -> `DefaultMessageStream.createMessageEvent()` stores it with `e.setBody(StringUtils.left(message, 2000))` -> legacy notification templates render `$messageEvent.body` directly. Relevant code paths: 1. Message submission: `Main/MessageSenderMacro.xml` sets `$message = "$!{request.messagestream_message.trim()}"` and then calls `$services.messageStream.postPublicMessage($message)`, `postPersonalMessage($message)`, `postMessageToGroup($message, ...)`, or `postDirectMessageToUser($message, ...)`, with only guest / CSRF / empty-message checks. 2. Storage: `DefaultMessageStream.java` `createMessageEvent()` contains `e.setBody(StringUtils.left(message, 2000));` and does not sanitize. 3. Web notification sink: `templates/notification/publicMessage.vm` calls `#displayMessageNotification(...)`; `templates/notification/macros.vm` macro `displayMessageNotification` contains `
$messageEvent.body
`. `groupMessage` uses the same pattern. 4. HTML email sink: `templates/notification/email/publicMessage.html.vm` calls `#displayMessageNotificationRightCell(...)`; `templates/notification/email/macros.vm` macro `displayMessageNotificationRightCell` contains `
$messageEvent.body
`. 5. Renderer behavior: `DefaultNotificationDisplayer.renderNotification()` calls `templateManager.execute(template)`. `InternalTemplateManager.getXDOM()` / `TemplateAsyncRenderer.renderVelocity()` wrap evaluated Velocity output in `RawBlock(result, targetSyntax)`. `InternalNotificationsRenderer.render()` then renders the notification block with `BlockRenderer` `html/5.0`. This preserves raw HTML output rather than escaping it. 6. Supporting framework evidence: XWiki rendering tests such as `DefaultWikiMacroTest` expect raw `html/5.0` content to stay as `onRawText [] [html/5.0]`, which is consistent with raw HTML preservation. Real-world trigger: an authenticated user can post ``. That payload is stored in the event body and later inserted into the notification HTML blockquote shown to recipients in the web notification UI, enabling stored XSS.

response.analysis: The report is a true positive. The source and sink are correctly identified, the path is reachable, and there is no sanitizer on the deprecation URL path. `UpdateSite.Data` accepts deprecation URLs verbatim from update-site metadata (`deprecations[*].url`) and also falls back to unvalidated `p.wiki` for plugins labeled `deprecated`. `PluginManager.doPluginsSearch` then embeds that value into `Messages.PluginManager_deprecationWarning(...)`, whose resource string contains raw HTML with `<a href="{0}">`. On the client side, the Handlebars template renders this field with triple-stash (`{{{ this.deprecated }}}`), and the generated rows are inserted via `insertAdjacentHTML`, so attacker-controlled quotes or markup are interpreted by the browser. This is a real CWE-079 sink, not a theoretical one. The path is runtime-reachable from the available-plugins UI and the endpoint is exposed to users with `SYSTEM_READ`. There is an explicit whitelist for the normal `wiki` field (`http/https` only), but no equivalent validation for `deprecated`, which shows the dangerous value is not sanitized before reaching the HTML sink. Default Jenkins deployments do reduce attacker control because update-site metadata is normally signature-validated and configuring a custom update site requires `ADMINISTER`, so exploitability is narrower than a fully unauthenticated XSS; however, that does not make the report false. Once malicious or compromised update-site metadata is accepted, Jenkins stores it and renders it unsafely, which is a real stored XSS / HTML injection issue. This matches the CWE-079 definition and does not rely on shallow or unreachable control-flow reasoning.
response.evidence: Source: `UpdateSite.java` stores deprecation URLs without validation: `String referenceUrl = entry.getString("url"); ... this.deprecations.put(pluginId, new Deprecation(referenceUrl));` and fallback `this.deprecations.put(p.name, new Deprecation(p.wiki));` ([UpdateSite.java:637-679]). The `Deprecation` type is just a raw `String url` holder ([UpdateSite.java:917-927]). Persistence: accepted update-site JSON is written to disk in `updateData` after optional signature verification, making this a stored payload ([UpdateSite.java:243-266]). Server-side sink construction: `PluginManager.doPluginsSearch` sanitizes `plugin.wiki` with an `http/https` check, but performs no such check for deprecations and instead does `jsonObject.put("deprecated", Messages.PluginManager_deprecationWarning(plugin.getDeprecation().url));` ([PluginManager.java:1522-1537]). HTML template: `PluginManager.deprecationWarning=...Learn more.` ([Messages.properties:94]). Client-side raw rendering: `{{{ this.deprecated }}}` in the available plugins template ([available.hbs:52-55]) and `tbody.insertAdjacentHTML("beforeend", rows);` in the UI code ([plugin-manager-ui.js:42-50]). Reachability: the JSON-producing endpoint is `doPluginsSearch` ([PluginManager.java:1479+]) and the page is accessible through `PluginManager`, whose target only checks `SYSTEM_READ` ([PluginManager.java:2690-2693]). Additional supporting evidence: Jenkins already has a security regression test for the related available-page update-center XSS class, `SECURITY-3037`, which disables signature checks, feeds malicious update-center metadata, and verifies the normal `wiki` link path no longer triggers script execution ([PluginManagerTest.java:838-861] and `security3037-update-center.json`). That test hardens `wiki`, but the deprecation path lacks the same validation.

response.analysis: The report correctly identifies a raw-HTML rendering path, but it does not establish a real attacker-controlled source in Jenkins' normal deployment model. The data flow is real: `UpdateSite.Plugin.excerpt` is copied into `/pluginManager/pluginsSearch`, rendered with `{{{ this.excerpt }}}`, and inserted with `insertAdjacentHTML`, so a hostile `excerpt` would execute if it reached this page. Therefore the report's payload assumes a malicious update-center entry without showing a realistic way for a low-privilege attacker to inject that entry into the trusted signed feed Jenkins actually consumes. This is a trust-boundary/source misidentification, not a demonstrated exploitable stored XSS in normal real-world use.
response.evidence: Real sink path: `UpdateSite.java` assigns `this.excerpt = get(o, "excerpt")`; `PluginManager.doPluginsSearch` does `jsonObject.put("excerpt", plugin.excerpt)`; `src/main/js/templates/plugin-manager/available.hbs` renders `{{{ this.excerpt }}}`; `src/main/js/plugin-manager-ui.js` inserts the HTML with `tbody.insertAdjacentHTML("beforeend", rows)`. There is no local sanitizer on that path. But the source is trusted/admin-controlled: `UpdateSite.updateData()` calls `verifySignatureInternal(o)` when `DownloadService.signatureCheck` is on; `DownloadService.signatureCheck` defaults to enabled; `JSONSignatureValidator` validates certificates against bundled `/WEB-INF/update-center-rootCAs`; and `PluginManager.doSiteConfigure()` requires `Jenkins.ADMINISTER` to change the update-site URL. Also, Jenkins' SECURITY-3037 hardening in `PluginManager.java` sanitized actually dangerous fields like `wiki` and `requiredCore` for this page while leaving `excerpt` as formatted update-center markup, consistent with treating it as trusted signed content rather than attacker-controlled input.

response.analysis: The sink is real, but the reported source is not a valid attacker-controlled source in XStream’s actual threat model. These files are standalone JMH benchmark launchers, not part of the main XStream library API or a server-side request path. Their environment inputs (`JAVA_EXE`, `JAVA_HOME`, `JDK_HOME`, `JAVA_OPTS`, `APP_OPTS`, and PATH fallback) are intentional operator/developer configuration for a locally executed benchmark ZIP. The control flow is direct and reachable, so this is not an A1/A2/D3 issue; the false positive comes from B1: treating invoker-controlled launcher configuration as attacker input. A real exploit would require some separate privileged wrapper/service to invoke this benchmark script while preserving an untrusted environment, and that privileged wrapper would be the vulnerable component, not XStream itself.
response.evidence: In `xstream-jmh/src/application/bin/xstream-jmh.sh`, the script explicitly documents that `JAVA_OPTS` and `APP_OPTS` 'can be set from outside' (line 19), clears inherited `JAVA_BIN` (`JAVA_BIN=` at line 20), derives the executable from `JAVA_EXE`/`JAVA_HOME`/`JDK_HOME` (lines 25-33), and finally executes `$JAVA_BIN $JAVA_OPTS -cp $APP_CP $MAIN_CLASS $APP_OPTS "$@"` (line 70). The Windows script does the same at line 63. However, project docs show this is only the benchmark artifact: `xstream-jmh/pom.xml` names it `XStream JMH Benchmark`; `xstream-distribution/src/content/download.html` lines 32-33 describe `xstream-jmh-...-app.zip` as a 'standalone application with start scripts'; `xstream-distribution/src/content/benchmarks.html` lines 27-32 tell users to unpack it and run the `bin` scripts locally, and even say they may swap libraries or add new benchmarks. That is developer-operated local tooling, not an exposed trust boundary. Also, the report overstates one detail: inherited `JAVA_BIN` is not trusted directly because the scripts reset it before use.

response.analysis: The reported path is security-relevant, but it is not a direct CWE-078 issue in this codebase. `XStreamer.fromXML()` does deserialize attacker-controlled XML in two stages: it first reconstructs an embedded `XStream` instance, then uses that reconstructed instance to deserialize the real payload. The path is real and reachable, and there is no sanitizer that strips dangerous embedded permissions. So the report correctly identifies a plausible unsafe-deserialization weakness. However, the sink in the shown code is deserialization (`readObject()`), not OS command construction/execution. Any eventual command execution would happen only indirectly through external gadget chains present on the runtime/classpath. That matches CWE-502/unsafe deserialization far better than CWE-078/improper neutralization in OS commands. Therefore, as a report for CWE-078, this is a false positive caused by CWE/prompt misalignment rather than bad dataflow reasoning.
response.evidence: Concrete path: `XStreamer.fromXML(Reader)` calls `fromXML(DefaultDriver.create(), xml)` and then `fromXML(driver, xml, PERMISSIONS)` at `xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/XStreamer.java:216-249`. In `xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/XStreamer.java:265-275`, it creates `outer`, adds bootstrap permissions, reads attacker-controlled config with `configIn.readObject()`, stores it as `XStream configured`, then immediately deserializes the payload with `configured.createObjectInputStream(reader)` and `in.readObject()`. The bootstrap permission set at `xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/XStreamer.java:54-70` explicitly allows the `TypePermission` hierarchy, so a serialized config can contain `AnyTypePermission`. `SecurityMapper.addPermission()` at `xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/mapper/SecurityMapper.java:64-68` clears previous restrictions when `AnyTypePermission.ANY` is added. The tests at `xstream/src/test/com/thoughtworks/acceptance/SecurityVulnerabilityTest.java:301-355` show that `AnyTypePermission.ANY` clears defaults and re-enables dangerous deserialization behavior that is otherwise blocked. XStream’s own advisory for gadget-based RCE (`xstream-distribution/src/content/CVE-2021-21345.html:23-31,97-101`) confirms that once restrictive type controls are absent, attacker-manipulated streams can trigger command-executing gadget chains. But there is no direct `Runtime.exec`, `ProcessBuilder`, shell invocation, or command-string construction anywhere in the reported `XStreamer` path, so the CWE-078 classification is the mismatch.

response.analysis:
The reported source and sink are correctly identified. `PdfDeliveryDispatcher.execute()` takes the suffix of `request.getRequestURI()`, splits it into `<key>/<filename>`, looks up the cached `PdfDelivery`, and if `delivery.getDirectory() != null` passes the request-controlled `filename` into `renderFile()`. `renderFile()` then does `new File(directory, filename)` and opens it with `new FileInputStream(file)` without any canonical-path check or rejection of `..` segments. `/pdfd/` is a real registered dispatcher, so this is not a hypothetical call path.

The report's concrete exploit story is too simplistic: the UUID key is not a normal long-lived user token. It is generated server-side with `UUID.randomUUID()` in `AbstractPdfSPI`, inserted into a cache only around the internal PDF-rendering request, and removed immediately after `render(...)` returns. So the statement that a normal user can simply obtain a valid key and replay `/pdfd/<key>/../../...` is overstated.

However, that does not make the finding a false positive. In supported HTML-certificate rendering flows, attacker-controlled HTML can be rendered under `/pdfd/<key>/...`. `UploadCertificateTemplateController` accepts ZIP-based HTML templates after only checking that a top-level `index.html` exists; `CertificatesManagerImpl.isHTMLTemplateAllowed()` allows HTML templates whenever PDF rendering is enabled; and `CertificatePdfServiceWorker` explicitly injects and relies on JavaScript (`qrcode.min.js`) inside the rendered HTML, which shows that script execution in the PDF-renderer context is expected. An attacker with permission to upload such a template can execute same-origin JavaScript during rendering, recover the live `/pdfd/<uuid>/...` path from `location`, request `/pdfd/<uuid>/../../...`, and place the returned file contents into the DOM/PDF output. That is a real arbitrary file-read risk, though with stronger prerequisites than the report claims.
response.evidence:
1. Reachable dispatcher and vulnerable sink:
- `src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/_spring/dispatcherContext.xml` registers `/pdfd/` -> `pdfDeliveryBean`.
- `PdfDeliveryDispatcher.execute()` parses `request.getRequestURI()` and sets `filename = uuid.substring(indexSuffix + 1)`.
- `PdfDeliveryDispatcher.renderFile()` uses `File file = new File(directory, filename);` followed by `new FileInputStream(file)` and copies the bytes to the HTTP response. There is no canonicalization or boundary check.

2. The UUID is transient, not a normal public token:
- `AbstractPdfSPI.convert(File path, ...)` creates `String key = UUID.randomUUID().toString();`, stores `PdfDelivery` in cache with `cache.put(key, delivery);`, calls `render(key, rootFilename, ...)`, then immediately `cache.remove(key);`.
- The internal renderers build the callback URL themselves: `GotenbergSPI` uses `Settings.getServerContextPathURI() + "/pdfd/" + key + "/" + rootFilename`, and `AthenaPdfSPI` does the same.

3. Real exploitability through supported attacker-controlled HTML:
- `UploadCertificateTemplateController.validateHtml(...)` only checks that the uploaded ZIP contains a top-level `index.html`; it does not sanitize HTML/JS.
- `CertificatesManagerImpl.isHTMLTemplateAllowed()` returns `pdfModule.isEnabled()`.
- `CertificatePdfServiceWorker.copyAndEnrichTemplate()` reads the HTML template, evaluates Velocity, writes it back, and `injectQRCodeScript(...)` inserts `<script src='qrcode.min.js'></script>` and inline JavaScript before rendering. This demonstrates that the HTML is executed in a browser-like PDF-renderer context.
- Because the malicious template is loaded from `/pdfd/<uuid>/<htmlFile>`, script in that page can derive the live UUID from `location.pathname`, fetch `/pdfd/<uuid>/../../../../...`, and render the fetched content into the generated PDF returned to the attacker.

response.analysis: The sink is real in isolation: `LocalFolderImpl.resolve()` delegates to `VFSManager.resolveFile()`, and that helper does not normalize or reject `..`. However, the report's claimed source is not raw attacker-controlled traversal input in the actual OpenOLAT request flow. `FolderMapper` is only exposed through the generic mapper framework, and all `/m/` mapper requests pass through `MapperDispatcher.execute()`, which rejects any mapper subpath containing `..` with HTTP 403 before `FolderMapper.handle()` is ever called. As a result, the concrete path claimed by the report (`user request -> FolderMapper.handle(relPath) -> container.resolve(relPath) -> VFSManager.resolveFile()`) is not exploitable for CWE-022 in normal product operation. This is therefore a false positive for the reported real-world vulnerability, even though the utility method would be worth hardening defensively.
response.evidence: `FolderMapper.handle()` directly calls `container.resolve(relPath)` in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/modules/bc/FolderMapper.java:45-48`. `LocalFolderImpl.resolve()` forwards to `VFSManager.resolveFile()` in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/LocalFolderImpl.java:469-476`, and `VFSManager.resolveFile()` indeed uses `new File(base, childName)` without rejecting `..` (`VFSManager.java:101-109` and `170-201`). But `FolderMapper` is only instantiated in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/modules/bc/FolderRunController.java:595` via `registerMapper(ureq, new FolderMapper(...))`. `BasicController.registerMapper()` uses the mapper service to generate a `/m/{id}` URL, and `MapperServiceImpl.register()` sets the URL under `DispatcherModule.PATH_MAPPED` (`/m/`). Every such request is handled by `src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/mapper/MapperDispatcher.java:73-112`, which computes the subpath `mod` and blocks it with `if (mod.indexOf("..") != -1) { ... 403 ...; return; }` at lines 105-109 before calling `m.handle(mod, hreq)`. Upstream path extraction in `DispatcherModule.subtractContextPath()` also URL-decodes the request URI when needed (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/DispatcherModule.java:107-130`). Therefore the reported attacker-controlled `relPath` containing `..` does not reach `FolderMapper` in the real HTTP flow.

response.analysis: The report is a true positive. The source and sink are correctly identified: `ItemExportServiceImpl.writeBitstreams()` reads `myName` from `bitstream.getName()` and uses it directly in filesystem operations (`mkdirs()`, `new File(destDir, myName)`, `createNewFile()`, `FileOutputStream`) without normalization or a canonical-path containment check. This is not a shallow or unreachable flow: DSpace persists bitstream names as metadata, and `Bitstream.setName()` performs no path validation. A supported REST upload path also accepts attacker-controlled names and stores them directly. There is no sanitizer on the export path, while the import side has an explicit `validateFilePath()` check, showing the missing export-side check is real. The main nuance is that the `export` script is admin-only by default, so a normal submitter cannot usually trigger export directly; however, this is still a real second-order CWE-22 because a malicious name can be planted by a user with upload/edit rights and later consumed by an administrator or privileged export job, causing writes outside the intended export directory under the DSpace service account's OS permissions.
response.evidence: In `ItemExportServiceImpl.writeBitstreams()`, the code does: `String myName = bitstream.getName(); ... if (myName.contains(File.separator)) { ... new File(destDir + File.separator + dirs).mkdirs(); } File fout = new File(destDir, myName); if (fout.createNewFile()) { InputStream is = bitstreamService.retrieve(c, bitstream); FileOutputStream fos = new FileOutputStream(fout); ... }`. There is no `normalize()`, `getCanonicalPath()`, or prefix check. In `Bitstream.java`, `setName(Context, String)` simply stores the provided string as metadata. In `BundleRestRepository.processBitstreamCreation()`, `bitstreamRest.getName()` is passed directly to `bitstream.setName(context, name)`, and otherwise `uploadfile.getOriginalFilename()` is used directly; this path does not use `Utils.getFileName()` to strip path components. The export path is reachable through the real `export` script (`ItemExport` / `ItemExportServiceImpl`), and the worker thread explicitly calls `context.turnOffAuthorisationSystem()` before exporting. By contrast, `ItemImportServiceImpl.validateFilePath()` resolves canonical paths and rejects files outside the parent directory, but no analogous guard exists in `writeBitstreams()`. Therefore a bitstream name like `../../../some/writable/location/payload` can escape `destDir` on typical Unix-like DSpace deployments and create/write a file outside the export package. `createNewFile()` prevents overwriting an existing file, but arbitrary new-file creation outside the intended directory is still a real CWE-22 security issue.

TP.report:
1
## Vulnerability 1 — `startsWith()` Without Path Separator Boundary Check (High)
**File:** `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:30-34`
```java
private static boolean isPathWithinFolder(String path, String folder) {
String rlatsPath = removeLeadingAndTrailingSlashesFrom(path);
String rlatsFolder = removeLeadingAndTrailingSlashesFrom(folder);
return rlatsPath.startsWith(rlatsFolder);   // ← FLAW
}
```
**Root cause:** `String.startsWith()` is a pure prefix match with no awareness of path separator boundaries. If `folder` = `/var/www/static`, then a path `/var/www/staticother/evil.txt` passes the check:
```
rlatsFolder = "var/www/static"
rlatsPath   = "var/www/staticother/evil.txt"
"var/www/staticother/evil.txt".startsWith("var/www/static") → TRUE  ← BYPASSED
```
**Exploitable path:** An attacker who can place a symlink inside the static files directory pointing to a sibling directory (e.g., `static/link → ../staticother`) would traverse into `staticother/`, and the `protectAgainstForExternal` check would accept the path as starting with the base folder prefix. The correct fix is to ensure the character immediately after the matched prefix is either a `/` or end-of-string.

response.analysis: TP. B1: the source and sink are correctly identified for the external static-file path. User-controlled request path data from `HttpServletRequest.getPathInfo()` reaches filesystem access for externally served static files, and the only intended guard is `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal()`. A1/A2/D3 do not explain this away: the path is reachable in normal use whenever an application enables `staticFiles.externalLocation(...)`, and no deeper project context blocks it. The key issue is a real normalization mismatch plus a bad containment check: `UriPath.canonical()` only canonicalizes `/`-based segments, but `StringUtils.cleanPath()` later converts `\\` to `/` and collapses `..`; after that, `isPathWithinFolder()` uses a raw `startsWith()` prefix test with no path-separator boundary. As a result, a request such as `/..\\static-secret\\secret.txt` against base `/var/www/static` becomes `/var/www/static-secret/secret.txt`, and the check incorrectly accepts it because that string still starts with `/var/www/static`. That is a real CWE-22 unauthorized file read outside the configured external static directory, limited to sibling paths whose names share the base-folder prefix. One nuance: the report's symlink example is not the best proof for this code because `ExternalResource.getPath()` is lexical rather than a resolved realpath, so the symlink story is incomplete; however the reported `startsWith()` flaw is still exploitable directly through crafted backslash traversal plus prefix-collision folders.
response.evidence: Source path: `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` reads `request.getPathInfo()` and builds `pathInContext` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59`). External resolution: `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` does `path = UriPath.canonical(path)`, `addedPath = addPaths(baseResource, path)`, `resource = new ExternalResource(addedPath)`, and if the file exists calls `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` before returning it (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:65-83`). `ExternalResource` constructs the backing file with `new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path))` and later serves it via `new FileInputStream(file)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-40,66-67`). The normalization mismatch is explicit in code: `UriPath.canonical()` only reasons about `/` delimiters (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:32-149`), while `StringUtils.cleanPath()` converts backslashes to `/` and resolves `..` (`src/main/java/spark/utils/StringUtils.java:218-266`). The guard is only `return rlatsPath.startsWith(rlatsFolder);` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:30-33`). Concrete exploit example: base `/var/www/static`, request `/..\\static-secret\\secret.txt` -> `addPaths` gives `/var/www/static/..\\static-secret\\secret.txt` -> `cleanPath` gives `/var/www/static-secret/secret.txt` -> `protectAgainstForExternal()` accepts it because `/var/www/static-secret/secret.txt` starts with `/var/www/static`. The file is then returned and streamed if readable in `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:89-98`). Existing tests do not refute this: `StaticFilesTestExternal` only creates `../dumpsterstuff` (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:84-91`), which does not share the `sparkish` prefix, and its traversal test therefore misses the prefix-boundary bug (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-125`).

response.analysis: B1: the source and sink are correctly identified. The source is the HTTP request path extracted in `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` and passed into the external static-file resolver. The sink is `new FileInputStream(file)` in `ExternalResource.getInputStream()`, whose contents are copied into the HTTP response. A1/A2: this is not a shallow or unreachable path; it is the normal external static-file serving flow. `MatcherFilter.doFilter()` invokes `staticFiles.consume()` before route handling, `ExternalResourceHandler` resolves the requested path, and if the resource exists it is returned and served. D1/D3: there is no sanitizer that resolves symlinks. `UriPath.canonical()` and `StringUtils.cleanPath()` only normalize `.` and `..`. The actual containment check in `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal()` uses `Paths.get(...).toAbsolutePath()` plus a lexical `startsWith` comparison, so a symlinked child path still appears to be inside the base directory even when its real target is outside. In a real deployment using `staticFiles.externalLocation(...)`, if the served tree contains a symlink such as `/var/www/static/link -> /etc`, a request for `/link/passwd` will pass the check and the framework will serve `/etc/passwd` (subject to process read permissions). This is a real path-restriction bypass causing arbitrary file read / information disclosure, so the report is a true positive.
response.evidence: `MatcherFilter.doFilter()` handles static files first and returns immediately if consumed (`src/main/java/spark/http/matching/MatcherFilter.java:97-101`). The request path is built from `servletPath` and `pathInfo` in `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-58`). `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` canonicalizes only `.`/`..` via `UriPath.canonical(path)`, then builds `addedPath = addPaths(baseResource, path)`, creates `new ExternalResource(addedPath)`, and on success calls `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:65-83`). That guard uses `unixifyPath()`, which is `Paths.get(path).toAbsolutePath().toString().replace("\\", "/")`, and `isPathWithinFolder()` is only a string-prefix check (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:18-33`). The same `ExternalResource` is later streamed in `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` via `resource.getInputStream()` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:91-103`), and the sink is `new FileInputStream(file)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-68`). No later code resolves the real path with `toRealPath()`, `getCanonicalFile()`, or `NOFOLLOW_LINKS`. The project’s test `StaticFilesTestExternal` shows `staticFiles.externalLocation(directoryRoot)` is normal supported usage and only tests `..` traversal blocking, not symlink escapes (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:74-125`).

## Vulnerability 2 — No Symlink Resolution (`toAbsolutePath` vs `toRealPath`) (High)
**File:** `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:26-28`
```java
private static String unixifyPath(String path) {
return Paths.get(path).toAbsolutePath().toString().replace("\\", "/");
//                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ resolves relative paths only, NOT symlinks
}
```
`Paths.get(path).toAbsolutePath()` does **not** resolve symbolic links. The correct method is `toRealPath()` (which calls `realpath(3)` internally). This means:
- If `/var/www/static/link` is a symlink → `/etc/`, then:
- `unixifyPath("/var/www/static/link/passwd")` = `/var/www/static/link/passwd`
- `isPathWithinFolder(...)` returns `true` (path starts with `/var/www/static`)
- But `getInputStream()` in `ExternalResource` opens `FileInputStream(file)` on the **real** filesystem path `/etc/passwd`
This is a complete bypass of the directory traversal check for external static file serving.
**Note:** This only applies to `protectAgainstForExternal`. For classpath resources (`protectAgainstInClassPath`), paths go through the JVM classloader, which is not filesystem-symlink aware in the same way.

response.analysis: The report does not demonstrate a real CWE-022 path traversal. The attacker-controlled source is the request path (`HttpServletRequest` path info), but the claimed sink is effectively the traversal guard itself, not an out-of-folder file read. In the real flow, Spark canonicalizes the URL path first, builds a resource path, normalizes the filesystem path again, and only serves the file if the resource exists and the guard passes. If the one-slash trim ever misclassifies a path, the code fails closed by throwing `DirectoryTraversalDetection`, which `StaticFilesConfiguration.consume()` converts into HTTP 400. That can at most reject a legitimate request; it does not let an attacker read a file outside the configured static directory. The report also assumes a raw `//...` path survives into `isPathWithinFolder` as `"/var/..."` versus `"var/..."`, but that does not match Spark's actual dataflow: external resources go through `new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(...))` and `Paths.get(...).toAbsolutePath()`, and classpath resources strip the leading slash before comparison. So the report is describing a hypothetical false-negative/rejection case, not a traversal exploit.
response.evidence: Dataflow for external static files: request path -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource()` combines `servletPath` and `pathInfo` ([src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59], [src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:80-119]) -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` canonicalizes with `UriPath.canonical(path)` and joins with `baseResource` ([src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:65-70], [src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:32-62]) -> `ExternalResource` creates `new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path))` ([src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-40], [src/main/java/spark/utils/StringUtils.java:218-266]) -> only if the file exists does Spark call `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` ([src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:81-83]). If that guard fails, Spark returns 400 instead of serving content ([src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:69-81]). For classpath resources, `ClassPathResource` removes the leading slash before storing the path ([src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResource.java:80-86]), so the report's `"/var/..." vs "var/..."` comparison does not reflect that flow. I also validated the runtime behavior in this environment (JDK 21/Linux): `new File("//var/www/static/file.txt").getPath()` and `Paths.get("//tmp/root").toAbsolutePath().toString()` both normalize `//` to `/`, so the report's double-leading-slash mismatch does not persist into the traversal check. Existing coverage also shows traversal attempts are rejected rather than served ([src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-127]).

response.analysis: The reported code pattern exists, but it is not a real CWE-022 vulnerability in Spark's actual request-serving logic. The report treats `ClassPathResource.createRelative()` and the protected constructor as if they were reachable from attacker-controlled HTTP paths, but in this codebase they are not. A repo-wide search shows no production caller of `createRelative()`; Spark's static-file handling instead uses `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource()`, which builds resources only through the public `ClassPathResource(String path)` constructor. That public constructor enforces `isValid(path)`, while the request path is also canonicalized by `UriPath.canonical()` and then checked by `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstInClassPath(...)` before any content is streamed. So the claimed bypass is a speculative API-level observation, not an exploitable path traversal in the real Spark code path.
response.evidence: 1. `createRelative()` is not used in the project request flow: `rg -n "createRelative\(" .` only finds `Resource.java`, `AbstractResource.java`, and `ClassPathResource.java` itself, with no production caller. 2. Real HTTP path flow is `StaticFilesConfiguration.consume(...)` -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59`) -> `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource(String)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:61-84`). 3. In that real flow, Spark canonicalizes the incoming path with `UriPath.canonical(path)` (`ClassPathResourceHandler.java:67`; see canonicalization logic in `src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:32-156`), then creates the resource with the public constructor `new ClassPathResource(addedPath)` (`ClassPathResourceHandler.java:69-71`), not the protected constructor. 4. The public constructor validates with `Assert.isTrue(isValid(path), "Path is not valid")` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResource.java:76-88`). 5. Even after resource creation, Spark enforces base-folder confinement with `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstInClassPath(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:82-84`; implementation at `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:12-15,30-33`). 6. The project has a regression test proving traversal attempts are blocked: `StaticFilesTest.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionLocal()` expects HTTP 400 for a traversal payload (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java:140-146`).

response.analysis: This is a false positive for CWE-022. The reported condition `path.contains("")` is indeed always true, but its actual effect is to make the normalization/traversal check unconditional, not to disable it. In `ClassPathResource.isInvalidPath`, the code always executes `StringUtils.cleanPath(path)` and then rejects cleaned paths containing `../`. In the real Spark request flow, user-controlled static-file paths are also canonicalized before `ClassPathResource` is created, and the resolved resource is then checked to ensure it still stays under the configured static root. So the report identifies a strange/dead condition, but not an exploitable path traversal. The tool missed the key sanitization/check points in the surrounding code path and treated a speculative missing null-byte check as a real traversal primitive.
response.evidence:
1) `ClassPathResource.isInvalidPath` does not become permissive because of `path.contains("")`; it becomes unconditional: lines 104-107 do `path = StringUtils.cleanPath(path); if (path.contains("../")) return true;`.
2) The only repository-reachable attacker-controlled path into `ClassPathResource` is static-file serving. `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource(String)` first does `path = UriPath.canonical(path);` before `new ClassPathResource(addedPath);` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java`, lines 67-71).
3) `UriPath.canonical` removes `.` / `..` segments and returns `null` when traversal would escape root (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java`). The tests confirm this behavior, e.g. `"/../foo" -> null` and `"/foo/../../bar" -> null` (`src/test/java/spark/resource/UriPathTest.java`).
4) After lookup, Spark enforces a root check: `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstInClassPath(resource.getPath(), baseResource);` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java`, lines 82-84). That check throws if the resolved classpath resource is not under the configured base folder (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java`, lines 12-15, 30-33).
5) The project already has a regression test showing traversal is blocked in practice: `StaticFilesTest.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionLocal` sends an encoded `..\\spark\\Spark.class` path and expects HTTP 400 (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java`, lines 141-145).
response.new_reason:: The report's null-byte theory is speculative. There is no evidence in this repository that the code was intended to be `path.contains("\0")`, and no demonstrated source-to-sink path showing that a NUL in a classpath resource name causes traversal through `ClassLoader.getResourceAsStream()` in Spark's actual static-file serving flow.

response.analysis: The report identifies a real data flow but the wrong sink semantics for the current project. User-controlled cron text can reach parser exceptions, and `CronValidator.isValid()` does pass `e.getMessage()` into `ConstraintValidatorContext.buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate()` (`src/main/java/com/cronutils/validation/CronValidator.java:32-33`). However, in this branch the project targets Jakarta Validation 3.1 (`pom.xml:80-83`) and its validation test uses `Validation.buildDefaultValidatorFactory()` with Hibernate Validator 9.0.1.Final (`src/test/java/com/cronutils/validation/CronValidatorTest.java:23`, `pom.xml:112-115`). In modern Hibernate Validator, custom violations created through `ConstraintValidatorContext` do not evaluate EL by default; EL must be explicitly enabled per-violation or globally. This repo has no such opt-in. So in the real code path here, the attacker can influence an error message, but not trigger EL execution or CWE-094. The report appears to rely on older/historical Hibernate behavior and the old issue history, not the current provider semantics.
response.evidence: Source path exists: parser exceptions embed user input in multiple places (`src/main/java/com/cronutils/parser/FieldParser.java:70,84,140,142,203,264,297`; `src/main/java/com/cronutils/parser/CronParser.java:150,165`) and that message reaches `buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate(e.getMessage())` (`src/main/java/com/cronutils/validation/CronValidator.java:32-33`). But the sink is not an active EL-execution sink in this project configuration. The repo test bootstraps the default validator (`src/test/java/com/cronutils/validation/CronValidatorTest.java:23`) and uses Hibernate Validator 9.0.1.Final (`pom.xml:112-115`). Hibernate Validator's official reference guide states that for custom violations, Expression Language is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled via `enableExpressionLanguage()` or `customViolationExpressionLanguageFeatureLevel(...)`: https://docs.hibernate.org/stable/validator/reference/en-US/html_single/ and https://docs.hibernate.org/validator/8.0/reference/en-US/html_single/. A repo-wide search shows no `enableExpressionLanguage`, no `customViolationExpressionLanguageFeatureLevel`, no `validation.xml`, and no provider bootstrap overriding that default. Therefore the reported path yields a validation error message, not code execution.
response.new_reason:: Even if a downstream application explicitly re-enabled EL for custom violations in modern Hibernate Validator, the default opt-in level is limited and the report's PoC assumes stronger EL capabilities than the current default behavior provides.
